37:
"And he cometh, and findeth them sleeping, and saith unto Peter, Simon, sleepest thou? couldest not thou watch one hour?" The witnesses note the tender reproach and Mark's distinctive form of it. The grammatical-historical Ellicott observes that "while St. Matthew and St. Luke give the question in the plural, St. Mark reports it in the singular, and joins it with the emphatic utterance of the name of the disciple" — and that Mark "includes the two questions which appear separately in the other two Gospels." Robertson presses the point: "Simon, sleepest thou? — the old name, not the new name, Peter. Already his boasted loyalty was failing in the hour of crisis; Jesus fully knows the weakness of human flesh."

The Wesleyan Wesley marks the irony of the address: Christ saith "to Peter — the zealous, the confident Peter." It was Peter who had so lately boasted that though all should be offended, he would not; and now the Lord finds him sleeping, and recalls him by his old name, Simon, the name of his natural weakness rather than the rock-name of his calling.

So the witnesses make the verse a poignant moment in Gethsemane. The Lord, returning from his agony, finds the three disciples — Peter, James, and John, the inner circle who had vowed to die with him — asleep; and to Peter, the most confident, he addresses the gentle but searching question, "Simon, sleepest thou? couldest not thou watch one hour?" The lesson the expositors draw is the contrast between the disciple's boast and his failure, and the Lord's tender awareness of human frailty. Mark's choice of the singular and the old name "Simon," the witnesses note, sharpens the rebuke: it falls on Peter by name, recalling him from his self-confidence to his weakness, even as Christ knows and pities that weakness. The "one hour" measures how slight the watch was that he could not keep — a small space of vigilance, asked at the hour of his Master's deepest need, and not given. The verse thus prepares for the denial to come, exposing in the sleeping Peter the same frailty of flesh that would soon, under greater pressure, deny the Lord he could not watch with for an hour.
38:
"Watch ye and pray, lest ye enter into temptation. The spirit truly is ready, but the flesh is weak." The witnesses read this as Christ's gracious counsel and his charitable excuse for the sleeping disciples. The Reformed Gill specifies the temptation in view: it was the temptation "of denying Christ, and falling off from him, which would quickly offer to them, when they should see him apprehended, bound, and led away." The watching and praying are the appointed defence against the trial about to break upon them.

The grammatical-historical Barnes connects the saying about spirit and flesh to Paul's analysis of the divided self — the inward man delighting in God's law, yet the flesh warring against it (Romans 7:18-25; Galatians 5:17). The willing spirit and the weak flesh together describe the condition of the disciples and of every believer.

So the witnesses make the verse the heart of Christ's Gethsemane charge to his followers. Knowing the temptation soon to assail them — the temptation to forsake and deny him — he bids them watch and pray, that they may not enter into it unguarded. And he tempers his reproof with a tender excuse: "the spirit truly is ready, but the flesh is weak." The lesson the expositors draw is twofold. First, the means of standing in trial: not self-confidence (which Peter had shown and would soon see fail), but watchfulness and prayer, the dependence on grace that alone can carry the soul through temptation. Second, the Lord's compassionate understanding of human frailty: he does not pretend the disciples are without fault, yet he names the willing spirit before the weak flesh, owning that their hearts were loyal even where their strength failed. The saying, the witnesses note, is at once a warning and a comfort — a warning that the readiest spirit, joined to weak flesh, will fall in temptation if it does not watch and pray; and a comfort that the Lord knows our frame, excusing where he might condemn, and pointing his stumbling disciples not to despair but to the watchfulness and prayer that would have kept them, and that may yet keep us, in the hour of trial.
39:
"And again he went away, and prayed, and spake the same words." The witnesses note briefly the Lord's return to his agony and the repetition of his prayer. Gill, who carries the comment, observes that he went "to the same place, or at much such a distance from them as before," and prayed "to the same purport as before." The repetition marks the depth and earnestness of the conflict in Gethsemane.

The expositors note that Christ, having found his disciples sleeping and recalled them to watchfulness, returns alone to wrestle in prayer, taking up again "the same words" — the petition that, if it were possible, the cup might pass, yet not his will but the Father's be done. The sameness of the words is itself significant: it shows that the agony was not eased at the first asking, but pressed upon him still, so that he must pray it again.

So the witnesses make the brief verse a window into the persistence of the Saviour's prayer in his hour of trial. He does not pray once and rise, but returns again, and "spake the same words" — repeating his submission as the conflict deepened. The lesson the expositors draw, here and in the parallel at Matthew, is the example of importunate prayer under the weight of suffering: Christ himself, in his greatest agony, prayed and prayed again, and was not ashamed to bring the same petition before the Father a second and a third time. The repetition teaches that there is no fault in praying the same request anew when the burden is not lifted; the Lord who bade his disciples watch and pray now shows them how, returning to the same words in the same submission, until the strength to drink the cup should be given. The sameness of the prayer, the witnesses note, is the measure of its sincerity and its struggle — not a vain repetition, but the renewed casting of an unbearable burden upon the Father, in a submission that grew firmer with each asking, until the will of the Father was wholly embraced. The verse, brief as it is, thus stands as a quiet example to all who pray under burdens too heavy to bear: that there is no weakness in praying the same petition again, and no unbelief in renewing a request the Father has not yet answered, but rather the faith that, like the Saviour's, holds on in submission until the strength to obey is given.
40:
"And when he returned, he found them asleep again, (for their eyes were heavy,) neither wist they what to answer him." The witnesses note the disciples' renewed sleep and their speechless shame, a detail peculiar to Mark. The grammatical-historical Barnes draws out the silence: "neither knew they what to answer him; they were so conscious of the impropriety of sleeping at that time, that they could not find any answer to give." Robertson observes Mark's vivid word for the heavy eyes (katabarynomenoi, "weighed down," "only here in the New Testament"), and notes that "they wist not what to answer him" is "alone in Mark and reminds one of the like embarrassment of these same three disciples on the Mount of Transfiguration" (Mark 9:6) — "on both occasions weakness of the flesh prevented their real sympathy with Jesus in his highest and deepest experiences."

The disciples, the expositors agree, were overcome not by indifference but by the weight of sorrow and weariness; yet their sleep, at such an hour, left them ashamed and dumb when the Lord returned. Their eyes were heavy, and "both their shame and their drowsiness would make them dumb" (Gould).

So the witnesses make the verse a further picture of human frailty in the presence of the Saviour's agony. Twice now the disciples have slept while Christ wrestled in prayer; and on returning he finds them asleep again, their eyes weighed down, and themselves unable to answer for it. The lesson the expositors draw is the persistence of the weakness Christ had just named — "the flesh is weak" — and the contrast between the Lord's deep travail and the disciples' inability to share it. Mark's detail, that "they wist not what to answer him," the witnesses note, is touchingly true to life: they had no excuse to offer, only the silence of shame; and Robertson's comparison with the Transfiguration is apt, for on both the highest and the darkest occasions of their Master's life the same three disciples failed to rise to the moment, overcome by the weakness of the flesh. The verse thus deepens the pathos of Gethsemane — the Lord bearing his agony alone, his nearest friends asleep and speechless beside him — and prepares for the hour when that same weakness would issue, in Peter, not in silence but in denial.
41:
"And he cometh the third time, and saith unto them, Sleep on now, and take your rest: it is enough, the hour is come; behold, the Son of man is betrayed into the hands of sinners." The witnesses note Mark's omission of the third prayer and the puzzling phrase "it is enough." The grammatical-historical Ellicott observes that Mark omits "the third repetition of the prayer," and that "it is enough" is "peculiar to St. Mark, probably noting the transition from the half-reproachful permission, Sleep on now, to the emphatic exclamation, the hour is come." Robertson explores the rare word apechei ("it is enough"), noting the papyri use it "as a receipt for payment in full," and that Jesus uses it "in an ironical sense, probably meaning that there was no need of further reproof" — "this is no time for a lengthened exposure of the faults of friends; the enemy is at the gate" (Swete).

Barnes offers another reading: "All is over; the time when you could have been of service to me is gone by." The witnesses note the tense of "is betrayed" — present, "is at this moment being betrayed" (Ellicott) — as Judas approaches. Cyril of Jerusalem draws the willingness: Christ "came to his passion willingly... lest the whole world should perish in its sins."

So the witnesses make the verse the close of Gethsemane and the turning toward the cross. The Lord comes the third time, and, with a word half-reproach and half-permission — "Sleep on now" — and then the startled "it is enough, the hour is come," announces that the time of watching is past and the hour of betrayal arrived. The lesson the expositors draw is the calm resolve of Christ as the crisis breaks. His agony in prayer is over; he rises tranquil, having submitted his will to the Father's, and turns to meet the betrayer, announcing his own delivery "into the hands of sinners." The disciples' opportunity to watch with him is gone, and he no longer reproves their sleep but accepts it — "it is enough" — for the time for vigilance has yielded to the time for action. The present tense, "is betrayed," the witnesses note, puts the betrayal already in motion: even as Christ speaks, Judas is at hand. The Saviour, who had wrestled and prayed, now goes forth composed to his Passion, the One who came willingly to die that the world might not perish in its sins.
42:
"Rise up, let us go; lo, he that betrayeth me is at hand." The witnesses read this brief command as the Lord's resolute movement to meet his betrayer. Gill, who carries the comment, paraphrases the spirit of it: "Rise up, let us go — to meet the enemy and the danger; for there is no escaping." Christ does not flee the approaching Judas but rises to go toward him.

The expositors note the contrast with all natural instinct. Where a man in danger would rise to escape, Christ rises to meet it — "let us go" not away from the betrayer but to him. The announcement "he that betrayeth me is at hand" is not the cry of alarm but the calm word of One who has foreseen and accepted his hour, and now advances to embrace it.

So the witnesses make the verse the final act of Gethsemane and the proof of the Saviour's willing passion. Having prayed and submitted, having found his disciples sleeping and roused them, Christ now rises — not to hide or to fly, for there is no escaping, nor would he escape if he could — but to go forward and meet the traitor whose approach he announces. The lesson the expositors draw, here and across this section, is the voluntariness of the Lord's death. He who foreknew the betrayal and could have avoided it goes out to meet it; "Rise up, let us go" is the word of One who lays down his life of himself, no man taking it from him. The very initiative is his: he does not wait to be seized but advances to be taken, turning the moment of his arrest into an act of self-surrender. The brief command, the witnesses note, gathers up the whole submission of Gethsemane into a single resolve — that the cup the Father had given, the cross that lay ahead, the betrayer now at hand, are all to be met, not fled; and so the Saviour rises from his agony to walk, with open eyes and willing heart, into the hands of the sinners for whom he came to die.
43:
"And immediately, while he yet spake, cometh Judas, one of the twelve, and with him a great multitude with swords and staves, from the chief priests and the scribes and the elders." The witnesses dwell on the betrayer and the armed crowd. The Latin Bede sets the scene against the Lord's tranquillity: "after our Lord had prayed three times, and had obtained that the fear of the Apostles should be amended by future repentance, he, being tranquil as to his Passion, goes to his persecutors." He notes the shame still clinging to Judas: "Judas had still something of the shame of a disciple, for he did not openly betray him, but by the token of a kiss." And he marks the absurdity the Lord will name: "it is foolish to seek with swords and staves him who offers himself of his own accord, and to search by night, as for one who hides, for him who taught daily in the temple."

The expositors note the aggravation of the phrase "one of the twelve" — that the betrayer was not a stranger but one of the chosen circle, who had eaten at the Lord's table. The "great multitude with swords and staves," sent by the whole Jewish authority (chief priests, scribes, elders), came armed against One who offered no resistance, as though to take a dangerous rebel by force in the night.

So the witnesses make the verse the arrival of the betrayer and the opening of the Passion's public scene. The lesson the expositors draw is the contrast between the malice of men and the calm of Christ. Judas, "one of the twelve," leads an armed band against his Master — the deepest treachery, by one of the inner circle, marked by Mark's pointed phrase; and the multitude comes with weapons by night, as against a robber, to seize One who had taught openly in the temple and now offered himself freely. The needless force, Bede notes, only exposes their folly and their wickedness: swords and staves against the willing Lamb, a traitor's kiss against the One who had loved him. Yet the verse also shows the steadiness of Christ, who, "tranquil as to his Passion," meets the crowd he had foretold; and it sets the stage for the kiss, the sword of Peter, and the flight of the disciples that follow, in which the malice of the betrayer, the violence of the mob, and the weakness of the friends are all gathered around the calm self-surrender of the Saviour.
44:
"And he that betrayed him had given them a token, saying, Whomsoever I shall kiss, that same is he; take him, and lead him away safely." The witnesses dwell on the treachery of the kiss and the detail peculiar to Mark. The grammatical-historical Robertson explains the "token" (syssēmon), "a concerted signal according to agreement... here only in the New Testament," and notes that "lead him away safely" is "only in Mark — Judas wished no slip to occur." The signal was the kiss, "a contemptible desecration of a friendly salutation." Barnes cross-references the treacherous kiss of Joab and the flattering words that are "smoother than butter" (2 Samuel 20:9; Psalm 55:21; Proverbs 27:6).

The Wesleyan Wesley draws out the poignancy: "probably our Lord, in great condescension, had used (according to the Jewish custom) to permit his disciples to do this, after they had been some time absent" — so that Judas turned the very token of a disciple's affection into the instrument of betrayal.

So the witnesses make the verse a study in the depth of the betrayer's treachery. Judas appoints the kiss — the customary greeting of love and reverence between disciple and master — as the sign by which the soldiers should know whom to seize; and he charges them to lead Christ away "safely," anxious that the prey not escape. The lesson the expositors draw is the peculiar baseness of betraying love. That Judas should use a kiss, Wesley notes, perhaps the very kiss Christ had graciously permitted his returning disciples, makes the treachery the more vile: the sign of affection becomes the badge of betrayal, the salutation of a friend the mark for the executioners. And his concern that Christ be led away "safely," the witnesses note (with Robertson), shows the cold deliberation of the traitor, who would leave nothing to chance. The kiss thus stands as the emblem of all false friendship — the smooth word and the embrace that hide a murderous intent (Psalm 55:21) — and sets in the sharpest relief the contrast between the betrayer's calculated treachery and the Lord's willing self-surrender, who would receive the kiss of Judas, as Bede said, not to teach deceit but lest he should seem to avoid betrayal.
45:
"And as soon as he was come, he goeth straightway to him, and saith, Master, master; and kissed him." The witnesses note the eagerness of the betrayer and the very word he spoke. The grammatical-historical Ellicott marks that "all the MSS. give the Hebrew word, and not its Greek equivalent" — "Rabbi, Rabbi" — preserving the actual syllable of the betrayer's salutation, doubled as if in feigned warmth. Barnes hears the false zeal: "Master, master — as if expressing great joy that he had found him again."

The expositors note the haste — "straightway" — with which Judas approaches and gives the appointed sign. He loses no time, but goes directly to Christ, hails him with the reverent title "Rabbi," and kisses him; the whole act of betrayal compressed into a moment of feigned affection. The doubled "Master, master" and the kiss together make the treachery the more glaring.

So the witnesses make the verse the consummation of the betrayer's signal. Judas, coming up, goes straight to his Master and performs the token he had arranged — a kiss, with the warm address "Rabbi, Rabbi." The lesson the expositors draw is the hypocrisy that clothes the deepest treachery in the forms of love and reverence. The betrayer does not openly accuse or seize; he greets Christ as his teacher, with the title of honour and the kiss of a friend, while delivering him to death. Mark's preservation of the very word "Rabbi," the witnesses note, gives the scene the vividness of an eyewitness memory and underscores the false piety of the act: the name of reverence on the lips of the traitor. The eagerness of his approach — going "straightway" to perform the sign — shows a heart settled in its purpose, no longer hesitating. And the kiss, given with the warmest greeting, becomes the very instrument of betrayal, so that the gesture of closest affection is made the mark for the soldiers' hands. The verse thus completes the portrait of Judas begun in the previous one: the disciple turned betrayer, who sells his Master with a kiss and a "Master, master," and in that moment of feigned devotion delivers the Lord of glory into the hands of sinners.
46:
"And they laid their hands on him, and took him." The witnesses note the seizure of Christ and the words that, in the other Gospels, preceded it. Gill records that the laying-on of hands followed Christ's words to Judas — "Friend, wherefore art thou come?" (Matthew) and "Judas, betrayest thou the Son of man with a kiss?" (Luke) — words of reproach and grief that Mark passes over, hastening to the act itself.

The expositors note the bare brevity of Mark's report: "they laid their hands on him, and took him." The armed multitude, guided by the kiss, now seizes the One they came for; the willing Lamb is taken. Mark records the fact without the surrounding detail the other Evangelists supply — the falling back of the crowd (John 18:6), the word to Judas — giving only the stark seizure.

So the witnesses make the verse the moment of the arrest, the hinge between the betrayal and the trial. The lesson the expositors draw is the willing submission of Christ to be taken. He who could have summoned twelve legions of angels, who in John's account had but to speak and the crowd fell back, suffers the hands of sinners to be laid upon him and offers no resistance. The bare statement — "they took him" — marks the beginning of the Passion proper: the free Lord of glory now a prisoner, delivered by a kiss into the power of his enemies. Mark's omission of the words to Judas, the witnesses note, fits his characteristic brevity, hastening the narrative toward the cross; yet the fuller reports of the other Evangelists, which Gill recalls, show that even in the moment of his arrest Christ spoke to his betrayer with a grief that exposed the treachery — "betrayest thou the Son of man with a kiss?" The seizure itself, however, Mark gives in its starkest form: the hands laid on, the Lord taken — the Shepherd struck, that the sheep, as he had foretold, would now be scattered. The hands laid on the Lord, the witnesses note, are the hands of the very sinners for whom he came to die; and his unresisting submission to their seizure is the first act of that surrender by which he would give his life a ransom for many, taken by force only because he willed to be taken.
47:
"And one of them that stood by drew a sword, and smote a servant of the high priest, and cut off his ear." The witnesses note Mark's anonymity about the swordsman and the fuller detail elsewhere. The grammatical-historical Robertson observes that "Mark does not tell that it was Peter — only John 18:10 does that, after Peter's death," and that "he really tried to kill the man, Malchus by name, as John again tells." Robertson adds that "Mark does not give the rebuke to Peter by Jesus" (Matthew 26:52). Gill identifies the swordsman as "Peter by name," one of the disciples standing by.

The witnesses, comparing the Gospels (Matthew 26:51; Luke 22:49; John 18:10), note the convergence of detail: a disciple, Peter, struck at the servant — aiming, Robertson judges, to kill — and cut off his ear. Mark and the other Synoptists leave the swordsman unnamed; only John, writing later, after Peter's death, supplies the name, both of the disciple and of the servant Malchus.

So the witnesses make the verse the record of Peter's rash blow in defence of his Master. The lesson the expositors draw, here and in the rebuke the other Gospels supply, is the contrast between the disciple's carnal zeal and the Lord's willing submission. Peter, who could not watch one hour, now draws the sword and strikes — a flash of the same impulsive courage that had boasted he would die with Christ — yet it was a zeal "not according to knowledge," resisting by force the very Passion Christ had come to embrace. Mark's reticence about the swordsman's name, the witnesses note (with Robertson), is significant: writing while Peter yet lived, the evangelist (on the traditional view, recording Peter's own preaching) does not expose him, where John, writing after Peter's death, freely names him. The detail of the severed ear, the witnesses observe, sets the stage for the healing Luke records and the rebuke Matthew gives — "put up thy sword" — by which Christ disowns the defence of the sword and reaffirms that he goes willingly to the cup the Father has given. The disciple's blow, however well-meant, only showed how little the Twelve yet understood the way of the Passion.
48:
"And Jesus answered and said unto them, Are ye come out, as against a thief, with swords and with staves to take me?" The witnesses note the Lord's rebuke of the manner of his arrest. The grammatical-historical Ellicott corrects the rendering: "as against a robber, the word implying the bolder form of theft," and Robertson adds that "highway robbers like Barabbas were common and often regarded as heroes" — noting the irony that "Jesus will be crucified between two robbers in the very place Barabbas would have occupied." Gill identifies the addressees as "the chief priests, and captains of the temple, and the elders, that came with the band."

The point of Christ's question, the expositors agree, is the absurdity and injustice of the armed midnight seizure. They came with swords and staves, as if to take a dangerous brigand by force; yet he was no robber, no man of violence, but the Teacher who had offered no resistance and threatened no one. The disproportion exposes the malice and the cowardice of his enemies.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's calm exposure of the wickedness of his arrest. The lesson the expositors draw is the contrast between the violence of his captors and the peaceableness of the Lamb. They came armed against him, "as against a robber," in the darkness and by stealth, treating the gentle Teacher as a hardened criminal who must be taken by main force. The very weapons they bore, the witnesses note, were a witness against them: they betrayed both the falseness of their charge — for he was no robber — and the cowardice of their proceeding, which dared not take him openly but came by night with a mob. Robertson's irony is pointed: the One seized "as against a robber" would shortly hang between two real robbers, in the place the robber Barabbas escaped. Christ's question, then, is not a plea for himself but a judgment on his enemies, laying bare the injustice of their armed assault upon One who had done no violence; and it prepares for the word that follows, in which he names the true reason for the night and the swords — that the Scriptures might be fulfilled.
49:
"I was daily with you in the temple teaching, and ye took me not: but the scriptures must be fulfilled." The witnesses note the contrast Christ draws and the reason he gives. The grammatical-historical Ellicott corrects "ye took me not" to "ye seized me not, ye laid no hold on me," and Gill marks the time: "I was daily with you in the temple — that is, for several days past, ever since he made his public entry into Jerusalem." Barnes cross-references the Scriptures fulfilled in his suffering (Psalm 22:1; Isaiah 53; Luke 24:44).

Christ's point, the expositors agree, is twofold. First, the cowardice of his enemies: he had taught openly in the temple day after day, where they might have taken him had their cause been just; yet they did not, but came by night with a mob — proof that they feared the people and knew their charge would not bear the light. Second, and deeper, the divine necessity: "the scriptures must be fulfilled." The night, the swords, the betrayal are not the triumph of his enemies' cunning but the working-out of what the Scriptures had foretold of the suffering Messiah.

So the witnesses make the verse the key to the whole arrest. The lesson the expositors draw is that the Passion, for all the malice and cowardice of men, proceeds according to the appointed counsel of God. Christ first shames his captors — he had been daily among them, teaching in the open temple, and they laid no hand on him; their resort to a midnight ambush convicts them of fear and injustice. But then he lifts the whole scene above their scheming: "the scriptures must be fulfilled." The betrayal, the armed seizure, the abandonment to his enemies are not accidents nor mere crimes but the fulfilment of prophecy — the suffering Servant of Isaiah, the forsaken sufferer of the Psalms, going the way that was written of him. The lesson the witnesses press is the submission of Christ to the Scriptures and to the Father's will: he does not resist his arrest, nor let Peter's sword defend him, because he knows that what is happening is the very thing the Scriptures foretold, and "must be fulfilled." The malice of men and the counsel of God meet at the cross; and Christ, exposing the one, bows willingly to the other, that the word might be accomplished.
50:
"And they all forsook him, and fled." The witnesses read this brief, sorrowful verse as the fulfilment of Christ's own prediction and the nadir of the disciples' weakness. Gill notes the textual clarity: "that is, his disciples, as the versions read... lest it should be thought the multitude were intended." Barnes cross-references the foretelling — "all ye shall be offended because of me this night" (Mark 14:27) — and the prophetic complaint of the forsaken sufferer (Psalm 88:8; Isaiah 63:3).

The expositors note the completeness of the desertion: "they all forsook him, and fled." Not one of the Twelve remained; the disciples who had vowed to die with him, who had drawn the sword in his defence, now, when the moment of danger came, abandoned him and ran. The flight fulfilled exactly what Christ had foretold that very night — that the Shepherd being struck, the sheep would be scattered.

So the witnesses make the verse the dark seal upon the disciples' failure and a fulfilment of prophecy. The lesson the expositors draw is the weakness of even the truest followers in the hour of trial, and the loneliness of the Saviour as he enters his Passion. The same disciples who had eaten with him at the supper, who had heard his prayers in Gethsemane, who had boasted their loyalty, now "all forsook him, and fled" — the boast of Peter and the rest dissolving in a moment before the armed crowd. The verse stands, the witnesses note, as the exact accomplishment of Christ's word — "ye shall all be offended" — and as a stroke of the prophetic portrait of the forsaken Messiah, abandoned by his friends and left alone. It marks the beginning of that solitude in which Christ would tread the winepress alone, none with him; and it sets the stage for Peter's denial, the deepest of the desertions, in which the foremost of the fleeing disciples would not merely abandon but deny his Lord. The flight of all is the measure of human frailty; the steadfastness of Christ, who goes on alone to the cross, is the measure of his faithful love.
51:
"And there followed him a certain young man, having a linen cloth cast about his naked body; and the young men laid hold on him." The witnesses canvass the identity of this mysterious young man, recorded by Mark alone. The grammatical-historical Barnes and Gill survey the guesses: "some think this was John... others James, the brother of our Lord; some that he was a young man of the house where Christ ate the passover, who had followed him to the garden"; but Gill judges "it seems most likely that he was one who lived in a house in or near Gethsemane, awaked out of sleep by the noise of the band of soldiers, who leaped out of bed and ran out in his shirt to know what was the matter." Barnes notes "it is omitted by all the other evangelists" and that "it is not known why this circumstance is recorded."

Lightfoot supplies the Jewish background of the sindōn (linen): the garment "wherewith he always went clothed was of sindon, that is, of linen," to which the fringes of the Law were fastened (Numbers 15:38) and with which men "covered their heads when they prayed." Bede had read the young man's flight allegorically and noted "that he was a young man at that time is evident from his long sojourn in the flesh" — a hint at the traditional identification with Mark himself.

So the witnesses make the verse a small, vivid, and much-debated detail of the arrest. The lesson the expositors draw, beyond the identity, is the universal terror of that night: even a chance follower, seized by the band, fled in panic, leaving his garment behind. The many guesses — John, James, a household youth, the man at whose house the supper was held, or (on an old tradition Bede hints at) the evangelist Mark himself, recording his own narrow escape — the witnesses leave unresolved, for the text does not say. What is clear is the scene: a young man, clad only in a linen sheet, following Christ when his disciples had fled, laid hold of by the soldiers, who would seize even him; and his flight, naked, into the night. The detail, peculiar to Mark, the witnesses note, has the unmistakable mark of an eyewitness memory — too particular and too pointless to be invented — and has long suggested that the evangelist set down, with characteristic reticence about the name, his own small part in that night of terror, when all forsook the Lord and fled.
52:
"And he left the linen cloth, and fled from them naked." The witnesses note the completion of the young man's flight and a homely parallel. Gill compares it to Joseph: "he left the linen cloth in their hands, just as Joseph left his garment in the hands of his mistress." Clarke supplies an Eastern custom: "the inhabitants of India... from their loose clothing can suddenly disengage themselves; when two Hindoos quarrel and one seizes the clothing of the other, the latter will leave his clothes in the hands of his opponent and flee away naked." Robertson notes the sindōn "was fine linen cloth used often for wrapping the dead" (Matthew 27:59; Mark 15:46) — "in this instance a fine sheet or even a shirt."

Bede (on v.43) had read the flight spiritually: the young man "fled from them whose presence and deeds he abhorred, not from the Lord, for whom his love remained fixed in his mind, when absent in body."

So the witnesses make the verse the close of the strange little incident. The young man, seized by the soldiers, slips out of the loose linen sheet they had grasped and escapes naked into the night, leaving the cloth in their hands. The lesson the expositors draw, beyond the vivid realism, is the terror that gripped all who were near Christ that night: even this chance follower, willing to follow when the disciples had fled, abandons his very garment to escape the band. The parallel with Joseph, who left his cloak in his temptress's hand and fled (Genesis 39), the witnesses note, lends the scene a touch of Scripture's own pattern of flight from danger; and Bede's reading, that the young man fled from the wicked crowd but not in heart from the Lord, gives it a gentler sense — the love that remains though the body flees. The detail, with the verse before it, the witnesses observe, bears every mark of an eyewitness's own recollection, set down with the characteristic Markan reserve about the name; and whether or not the young man was Mark himself, the incident stands as a small, true memory of that night of universal flight, when even the loosely-clad follower escaped naked from the hands that had laid hold of the Lord.
53:
"And they led Jesus away to the high priest: and with him were assembled all the chief priests and the elders and the scribes." The witnesses note the convening of the Sanhedrin and the figures around the fire. The Latin Bede identifies "the high priest, Caiaphas, who was high priest that year, of whom Josephus relates that he bought his priesthood of the Roman Emperor," and the whole council assembled — "all the chief priests, elders, and scribes." He reads Peter's following "afar off" as itself the seed of denial: "rightly does he follow afar off who is just about to betray him, for he could not have denied Christ if he had remained close to him."

Bede also allegorises the fire at which Peter warmed himself: there is the fire of charity (Luke 12:49) and "a fire of covetousness" (Hosea 7:4); "this fire, raised in the hall of Caiaphas by the suggestion of an evil spirit, was arming the tongues of the traitors to deny and blaspheme the Lord." Peter, "benumbed by the cold" — "because the abounding of iniquity, the love of many waxes cold" (Matthew 24:12) — "sought in the society of traitors the consolation of worldly comfort." Pseudo-Jerome reads "the hall of the High Priest" as "the enclosure of the world, the servants are the devils, with whom whosoever remains cannot weep for his sins."

So the witnesses make the verse the opening of the night trial and the setting of Peter's fall. The lesson the expositors draw, with Bede, is the danger of following Christ "afar off" and of seeking warmth among his enemies. The whole council gathers in the high priest's house, bent on condemning Christ; and Peter, having recovered enough courage to follow, yet not enough to stand openly with his Master, comes in "afar off" and sits among the officers at their fire. The very distance and the very company, Bede notes, were the predisposing causes of his denial: he who would not be near Christ, and who warmed himself with the servants of Caiaphas, had put himself in the way of falling. The fire, the witnesses note, becomes in the fathers' reading a figure of the choice before every disciple — the fire of charity, which Christ came to kindle, or the cold of an iniquitous world, in which love grows cold and the tongue is armed to deny. Peter chose, for the moment, the wrong fire; and the scene is set both for the trial of the Master within and the denial of the disciple without.
54:
"And Peter followed him afar off, even into the palace of the high priest: and he sat with the servants, and warmed himself at the fire." The witnesses dwell on Peter's furtive following and the firelight that would betray him. The grammatical-historical Robertson draws out the picture: Mark uses "the constative aorist (followed) where Matthew and Luke have the picturesque imperfect (was following); possibly Mark did not care to dwell on the picture of Peter furtively following at a distance, not bold enough to take an open stand with Christ as the Beloved Disciple did, yet unable to remain away." He notes the "periphrastic imperfect, picturing Peter making himself at home with the officers," and — crucially — "warming himself in the light (pros to phōs): fire has light as well as heat, and it shone in Peter's face; he was not hidden as much as he supposed."

Ellicott and Clarke press the same point: "at the light" (not merely "at the fire"), "the word bringing out very vividly the effect of the glare of the charcoal fire on St. Peter's face," by which "the maid servant was the better able to distinguish him" (Mark 14:67). Gill notes another disciple, "perhaps John," went in with Jesus, and by his means Peter "afterwards got in."

So the witnesses make the verse the immediate prelude to Peter's denial, full of vivid and ominous detail. The lesson the expositors draw is the false security of half-discipleship. Peter follows, but "afar off" — neither boldly with Christ nor safely away, but in the dangerous middle, drawn by love yet held back by fear. And he sits down among the very officers who had arrested his Master, warming himself at their fire — seeking, as Bede said, worldly comfort in the company of enemies. The detail Mark and the others preserve, the witnesses note, is charged with irony: Peter thought to hide in the crowd, but the firelight he sought for warmth fell full on his face, and would soon expose him to the maid. The "light" of the fire, Robertson observes, betrayed the man who imagined himself concealed. The verse thus sets the trap: Peter, following afar, seated among foes, lit up by the fire he warmed himself at, is now where his courage will be tested and found wanting — the boastful disciple about to deny, in the glare of the very fire he chose, the Lord he had vowed to die for.
55:
"And the chief priests and all the council sought for witness against Jesus to put him to death; and found none." The witnesses note the predetermined verdict and the providence that frustrated it. The Wesleyan Wesley marvels: "what an amazing proof of the overruling providence of God, considering both their authority and the rewards they could offer, that no two consistent witnesses could be procured to charge him with any gross crime." Gill notes the chief priests "were of all most busy and active in this matter."

The expositors mark the injustice laid bare: the council did not weigh evidence to reach a verdict, but "sought for witness" to support a verdict already fixed — "to put him to death." Theirs was not a trial but a search for a pretext; yet, with all their authority and their power to reward false testimony, they "found none" that would serve.

So the witnesses make the verse the exposure of a corrupt tribunal and a testimony to the innocence of Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is twofold. First, the malice of the council, which had condemned Christ before it tried him, and now sought only the witnesses that might give their murder the colour of law. Second, and more wonderful, the overruling providence of God, which Wesley presses: that a body so powerful, so determined, and so able to bribe, could not find two agreeing witnesses to fasten any crime upon Jesus. The failure, the witnesses note, is itself a divine attestation of the Lord's perfect innocence: the most hostile court, with every advantage and every incentive to convict, could produce no consistent charge against him, so spotless was the life they sought to condemn. The very corruption of the proceeding — seeking witness to kill rather than truth to judge — turns, in God's providence, into a vindication of Christ; for where no honest charge could be found, and only false and disagreeing testimony offered, the innocence of the accused stands the more gloriously confirmed. The council's failure to find a witness is the silent witness of God to the sinlessness of his Son.
56:
"For many bare false witness against him, but their witness agreed not together." The witnesses note the abundance of false testimony and its self-defeating disagreement. The grammatical-historical Robertson draws out the Greek: "the testimonies were not equal — they did not correspond on essential points"; "many were bearing false witness (imperfect, repeated action)," yet "no two witnesses bore joint testimony to justify a capital sentence according to the law" (Deuteronomy 19:15) — "note the imperfects to indicate repeated failures." Lightfoot supplies the rabbinic law of testimony, by which witnesses were rigorously examined for agreement, and marvels that, had such scrutiny been honestly applied, "the unspeakable innocence of the most blessed Jesus, which envy itself could not have fastened any crime upon," would have shone forth.

The Wesleyan Wesley glosses "agreed not together" as "were not equal — not equal to the charge of a capital crime," the same word recurring in v.59. The many false witnesses, that is, contradicted one another, so that even by the forms of their own law no valid charge could be built.

So the witnesses make the verse a further proof of the corruption of the trial and the innocence of Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the providence that turned the very falseness of the testimony against itself. The council found, not too few witnesses, but too many — "many bare false witness" — yet their lies, being lies, would not cohere: "their witness agreed not together." The malice that could procure false testimony in abundance could not make it consistent; and the law's own requirement of agreeing witnesses (Deuteronomy 19:15), which the council was bound to honour, defeated their purpose. Lightfoot's reflection, the witnesses note, is searching: had the rabbinic rules for examining witnesses been honestly kept, the perjurers would have been exposed and the spotless innocence of Jesus made manifest; envy itself could fasten no crime upon him. The disagreement of the false witnesses, then, is no mere accident but a divine safeguard, by which God preserved the testimony to his Son's innocence even in the midst of a court bent on his death. The Lord is condemned at last, not on agreeing evidence, but on his own confession; and the failure of the false witnesses stands as the abiding monument to his sinlessness.
57:
"And there arose certain, and bare false witness against him, saying..." The witnesses note this fresh attempt at testimony and Mark's reticence. The grammatical-historical Robertson reads it as "a desperate attempt once more (conative imperfect)" — the council, having failed to find agreeing witnesses, now sees "certain" rise to offer the charge about the temple. Ellicott observes that "St. Mark is here less definite than St. Matthew, who, writing for Jews, was apparently anxious to show that the rule which required two or three witnesses had barely been complied with." Gill notes that these were "two false witnesses, who stood up in court; for witnesses were obliged to stand whilst they gave in their testimony."

The expositors mark the persistence of the malice. The general false witness having failed for want of agreement, certain particular witnesses now rise to bring the one charge that seemed most promising — the saying about destroying the temple — in a fresh effort to find a capital crime.

So the witnesses make the verse the introduction of the temple-charge, the last and best hope of the false accusers. The lesson the expositors draw is the determination of the council to convict at any cost. Having found the mass of false testimony useless because it would not agree, they fasten now on a specific charge — the words about the temple — brought by "certain" who rise to swear it. Mark, the witnesses note (with Ellicott), is less precise than Matthew about the number of these witnesses, Matthew being concerned to show that the legal minimum of two was just met; but both record the same desperate manoeuvre. The rising of these witnesses, Robertson observes, is a "conative" effort — an attempt that, as the next verses show, would also fail, for even this charge they could not make to agree. The verse thus continues the portrait of a court grasping for a pretext: false witness in abundance having failed, particular witnesses are now suborned to twist a remembered saying of Christ into a capital crime — a manoeuvre that would itself collapse, leaving the council, at the last, with no recourse but to extract from Christ's own lips the confession on which they would condemn him.
58:
"We heard him say, I will destroy this temple that is made with hands, and within three days I will build another made without hands." The witnesses examine the famous distortion and the truth hidden in it. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the antithesis "made with hands... made without hands" is "in Mark alone," and that "it is possible that a real saying of Jesus underlies the perversion" — the saying of John 2:19, where Christ "referred not to the temple in Jerusalem, but to the temple of his body, though no one understood it at the time." Swete (cited by Robertson) observes that "Jesus did do what he is quoted as saying: his death destroyed the old order, and his resurrection created the new — but these witnesses did not mean that." Ellicott traces an echo of the antithesis in "the more perfect tabernacle, not made with hands" (Hebrews 9:11). Bede (on v.53) had judged: "he is a false witness who understands words in a sense in which they are not spoken."

The Wesleyan Wesley marks the significance of the date: "the words which they thus misrepresented were spoken by Christ at least three years before (John 2:19); their going back so far to find matter for the charge was a glorious, though silent, attestation of the unexceptionable manner wherein he had behaved through the whole course of his public ministry."

So the witnesses make the verse a study in the perversion of Christ's words and the truth they unwittingly bore. The lesson the expositors draw is twofold. First, the nature of false witness: not always the invention of what was never said, but the twisting of true words "in a sense in which they are not spoken" (Bede). Christ had said, "Destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up" (John 2:19), speaking of his body; the witnesses make him say he would himself destroy the temple in Jerusalem and rebuild it — a malicious distortion of a saying about his death and resurrection. Second, the providence that wrung a testimony to his innocence even from his accusers: that they had to reach back three years for matter, Wesley notes, was itself a silent witness to the blamelessness of his whole ministry. And the irony the witnesses press, with Swete, is profound — the very saying they perverted was true in the deepest sense: Christ would indeed, by his death and rising, destroy the old temple-order and raise the new, the tabernacle "not made with hands"; the false witnesses spoke, without knowing it, the truth of the gospel they meant to use against him.
59:
"But neither so did their witness agree together." The witnesses note that even the temple-charge, the council's best hope, collapsed for want of agreement. The grammatical-historical Ellicott observes that this is "peculiar to St. Mark," who adds the point absent from Matthew: "we are not told in what respects their evidence failed to agree; possibly in details of time and place, possibly in the absence or presence of the words reported." Robertson (on v.58) had noted Mark "repeats the point of verse 57" — that "not even so did their witness agree together." Gill explains: "their witness did agree together, for they both witnessed the same thing; but not so as to found upon it the charge of a capital crime — their witness was not equal, not sufficient to convict him."

The expositors mark the final frustration of the false accusers. Even the witnesses to the temple-saying, on whom the council had pinned its hope, could not make their testimony cohere closely enough to ground a capital sentence; and so this charge too failed.

So the witnesses make the verse the collapse of the last attempt to convict Christ by false testimony. The lesson the expositors draw is the same divine providence pressed throughout the trial: that God would not suffer his innocent Son to be condemned on agreeing evidence, but caused even the most promising false charge to fall apart. Mark's repetition of the point — that "neither so did their witness agree" — the witnesses note, drives home that the failure was total: not the general false witness only, but even the specific and best-prepared charge about the temple, broke down for lack of the agreement the law required. Gill's careful distinction is instructive: the witnesses did agree in substance, both reporting the temple-saying, but not "equally," not closely enough in the particulars to satisfy the legal standard for a death sentence. The council is thus left, at the close of its search, with no valid charge whatever — every false witness self-defeated, every distortion frustrated. The innocence of Christ stands unimpeached by any evidence; and the high priest, baffled, will now abandon the pretence of testimony and turn to interrogate Christ himself, seeking from his own mouth the confession that the witnesses could not supply.
60:
"And the high priest stood up in the midst, and asked Jesus, saying, Answerest thou nothing? what is it which these witness against thee? But he held his peace, and answered nothing." The witnesses note the high priest's angry impatience and the Lord's deliberate silence. The Latin Bede reads the rising: "the more Jesus remained silent before the false witnesses unworthy of his answer, the more the high priest, overcome with anger, endeavoured to provoke him to answer, that he might find room for accusing him from anything he might say." The high priest "rises from his seat, thus shewing by the motion of his body the madness of his mind."

Pseudo-Jerome reads the silence prophetically: Christ, "led as a sheep to the slaughter, without crying, remained mute and kept silence yea even from good words" (Isaiah 53:7); "the silence of Christ is the pardon for the defence or excuse of Adam" (Genesis 3:10). The Lord's refusal to answer the false witnesses, the witnesses agree, was deliberate: their testimony was "unworthy of his answer" (Bede), and to dispute it would only feed the high priest's design.

So the witnesses make the verse the meeting of the high priest's frustrated rage with the majestic silence of Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is twofold. First, the desperation of the accusers: the high priest, having failed to convict by witnesses, stands up in anger and tries to provoke Christ into some self-incriminating word — "Answerest thou nothing?" — betraying, Bede notes, "by the motion of his body the madness of his mind." Second, the deliberate and prophetic silence of the Lord: he "held his peace, and answered nothing," fulfilling the portrait of the silent Sufferer of Isaiah 53, who opened not his mouth. The silence, the witnesses press, was not weakness but wisdom and submission: the charges were too contradictory to require refutation, and to answer would have served only the high priest's purpose of finding matter for accusation. Pseudo-Jerome's reading deepens it — the silence of the second Adam atoning for the excuses of the first, who hid and made his plea in Eden. So Christ, accused falsely, stands silent before his judges, neither defending himself against testimony that condemned itself nor giving his enemies the word they sought; and his silence, the witnesses note, is itself a fulfilment of Scripture and a part of his willing passion, until the high priest, foiled in this too, will put the one question that Christ will not leave unanswered.
61:
"But he held his peace, and answered nothing. Again the high priest asked him, and said unto him, Art thou the Christ, the Son of the Blessed?" The witnesses dwell on the silence broken at last by the decisive question. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes that "Mark adds the negative statement to the positive 'kept silent' — and answered nothing," and that "Mark does not give the solemn oath in Matthew under which Jesus had to answer." Barnes anchors the silence in prophecy (Psalm 39:9; Isaiah 53:7; 1 Peter 2:23). On "the Son of the Blessed," Ellicott explains that where Matthew and Luke have "the Son of God," Mark gives "the Blessed" — "the use of the Blessed as a name of God in doxologies and solemn formulae was a common practice"; Clarke notes that "of God the Blessed One" is "a frequent Hebrew form of speech."

The expositors mark the turning-point. The Lord, silent before the false witnesses, is now directly asked, under oath (as Matthew records), the one question he will not evade: whether he is the Christ, the Son of God. To this he must answer, for it touches not a false charge but the very truth of his Person.

So the witnesses make the verse the hinge of the trial. The lesson the expositors draw is the difference between the silence and the confession of Christ. He held his peace before the lying witnesses, refusing to dignify their contradictions with a reply; but when the high priest, abandoning the failed testimony, puts the direct question — "Art thou the Christ, the Son of the Blessed?" — the Lord will not be silent, for here is no false accusation to be ignored but the central truth to be confessed. The high priest's title "the Son of the Blessed," the witnesses note (with Ellicott), uses the reverent Jewish circumlocution for God, so that the question is plainly whether Jesus claims divine Sonship — the very claim for which they would condemn him as a blasphemer. Robertson observes that Mark, characteristically brief, omits the solemn adjuration under which (in Matthew) the question was put; but the force is the same. The Lord's silence and his coming confession together, the witnesses press, reveal his wisdom: he would not defend himself against falsehood, but he would not deny the truth of who he was, though that truth would be the ground of his condemnation. The question is asked; and in the next verse Christ will give the answer that seals his death and proclaims his glory.
62:
"And Jesus said, I am: and ye shall see the Son of man sitting on the right hand of power, and coming in the clouds of heaven." The witnesses fasten on the unambiguous confession and the majestic claim. The grammatical-historical Robertson presses the directness: "I am (egō eimi). Matthew has 'Thou hast said,' which is the equivalent of the affirmative; but Mark's statement is definite beyond controversy." Where Matthew's form is an affirmation drawn out, Mark gives the plain, bold "I am" — a clear and open avowal of his Messiahship and divine Sonship. Barnes cross-references the great prophecy Christ here claims (Daniel 7:13; Matthew 24:30; 26:64). Cyril of Jerusalem gathers the apostolic witness to the Son's session "on the right hand of power" (Acts; Romans 8:34; Ephesians 1:20; Colossians 3:1; Hebrews).

The Lord, having confessed his Person, adds his vindication: the very judges who now condemn him shall one day see him exalted — "sitting on the right hand of power, and coming in the clouds of heaven" — the language of Daniel's Son of man, claiming both present session and future advent in glory.

So the witnesses make the verse the climax of the trial and the great self-revelation of Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the boldness and the fulness of his confession. Asked plainly whether he is the Christ, the Son of the Blessed, he answers plainly — "I am" — Mark's form (the witnesses note, with Robertson) being even more direct than Matthew's, leaving no room for doubt that Christ openly claimed to be the Messiah and the divine Son. And he does not stop at the bare confession but joins to it the prophecy of Daniel 7:13: that his judges shall see the Son of man enthroned at God's right hand and coming on the clouds — a claim at once to present heavenly authority and to future judicial glory. The One they try as a prisoner declares himself their coming Judge. The confession, the witnesses press, is the very thing for which they will condemn him as a blasphemer; yet it is the truth, openly avowed at the cost of his life. Christ, silent before false witness, speaks plainly before the direct question, sealing his own death-sentence with the confession of his deity — and turning the moment of his deepest humiliation into the proclamation of his highest glory, the suffering prisoner declaring himself the enthroned Son of man who will come in the clouds of heaven.
63:
"Then the high priest rent his clothes, and saith, What need we any further witnesses?" The witnesses note the high priest's dramatic gesture and its meaning. The grammatical-historical Ellicott observes a precise distinction: "St. Mark uses the word for the inner garment, St. Matthew that for the outer." Gill explains the act as customary "upon hearing blasphemy, which he now supposed the case, or at least would have it so thought." Barnes cross-references the prophetic irony (Isaiah 58:1).

The rending of the garments, the expositors agree, was the recognised Jewish sign of horror at blasphemy. The high priest, hearing Christ's confession that he was the Son of God, treats it as blasphemy and rends his clothes in token of holy outrage — and seizes upon it as the long-sought ground of condemnation: "What need we any further witnesses?" The failed search for testimony is now abandoned; Christ's own words supply the charge.

So the witnesses make the verse the high priest's seizing of the confession as blasphemy. The lesson the expositors draw is the irony and the injustice of the gesture. The rending of the garments, meant to express abhorrence of blasphemy, becomes instead the sign of the council's own guilt — for the words they called blasphemy were the truth, the confession of the true Son of God; and the high priest, in tearing his robe, unwittingly enacted (as Bede noted on v.60) a figure of the rending of the Jewish priesthood itself, soon to be torn away for the wickedness of the priests. The high priest's relief is evident: the trial that had failed for want of witnesses is now resolved by Christ's own confession — "what need we any further witnesses?" The accusers, foiled by the disagreement of every false testimony, find in the Lord's plain avowal the charge they could not otherwise secure. Yet the witnesses press the deeper truth: that the "blasphemy" was no blasphemy but the truth divinely confessed, and that the high priest, in his show of zeal for God's honour, was condemning the very Son of God. The torn garment, the witnesses note, is the emblem of a priesthood and a nation rejecting their Messiah in the name of the God who sent him — outraged at a truth they called blasphemy, and rending their robes over the confession of the One whose seamless garment the soldiers would not tear.
64:
"Ye have heard the blasphemy: what think ye? And they all condemned him to be guilty of death." The witnesses note the verdict and a telling inference about who was present. The grammatical-historical Ellicott explains the old usage of "guilty of death": "the translators connect 'guilty' not, as we now do, with the crime, but with the punishment to which a man is liable." Robertson draws an inference from "they all": "this would mean that Joseph of Arimathea was not present, since he did not consent to the death of Jesus (Luke 23:51); Nicodemus was apparently absent also, probably not invited because of previous sympathy (John 7:50); but all who were present voted for the death of Jesus." Gill notes the high priest's appeal to "the manifest blasphemy... out of his own mouth."

The verdict, the expositors agree, was unanimous among those present: hearing the confession the high priest had branded blasphemy, "they all condemned him to be guilty of death." The trial that could find no agreeing witness ends in agreeing condemnation, on the ground of Christ's own words.

So the witnesses make the verse the formal condemnation of the Son of God. The lesson the expositors draw is the unanimous guilt of the assembled council and the providential absence of the few who might have dissented. The high priest, having pronounced the confession blasphemy, puts it to the vote — "what think ye?" — and the answer is one: "they all condemned him to be guilty of death." The "all," Robertson notes, is significant: it implies the absence of Joseph of Arimathea and Nicodemus, the secret disciples, who did not consent to the deed; so that those who voted were the hostile majority, and their verdict was complete. The condemnation, the witnesses press, rests not on any proven crime — for none was found — but solely on Christ's own confession that he was the Son of God; the council condemns him, in effect, for telling the truth about himself. The phrase "guilty of death," in its older sense, means liable to the death-penalty: the Sanhedrin judges the Son of God worthy to die for confessing his deity. The injustice is total, yet the verdict, the witnesses note, sets in motion the very Passion Christ had foretold and willingly embraced — condemned by the rulers of his people, that he might give his life a ransom for the many they refused to acknowledge him among.
65:
"And some began to spit on him, and to cover his face, and to buffet him, and to say unto him, Prophesy: and the servants did strike him with the palms of their hands." The witnesses note the indignities heaped on the condemned Christ and the point of the taunt. The grammatical-historical Ellicott explains the covering of the face, "recorded by St. Mark and St. Luke but not by St. Matthew, which gave point to the taunt Prophesy: they blindfolded the Prophet, and then called on him to use his power of supernatural vision." Robertson describes the "veil around his face," the jeering demand "Prophesy" (meaning, as Matthew and Luke add, to tell who struck him while blindfolded), and the blows — rapismata, "to slap the face with the palm," "a gross indignity." Gill notes the spitting fulfilled prophecy (Isaiah 50:6; and the smiting, Isaiah 1:6).

The expositors mark the two forms of outrage — spitting and buffeting, the clenched fist and the open palm — and the mockery of his prophetic office: blindfolded, struck, and challenged to prophesy who had struck him. The "servants" or officers, who had arrested Jesus and still held him, now abuse him with their hands.

So the witnesses make the verse the beginning of the physical abuse of the condemned Saviour, foretold by the prophets and aimed at his very office. The lesson the expositors draw is the depth of the shame Christ bore and the fulfilment of Scripture in it. The men spit in the face of the One who had healed the blind; they cover his face and buffet him; they mock his prophetic claim, blindfolding him and demanding "Prophesy" — tell who struck you — as if to expose the powerlessness of the Prophet they had condemned. The blindfold, the witnesses note (with Ellicott), is what gives the taunt its point: they challenge the supernatural sight of the One they cannot see to be the Son of God. Every indignity, Gill observes, fulfils the prophetic portrait of the suffering Servant — "I gave my back to the smiters, and my cheeks to them that plucked off the hair; I hid not my face from shame and spitting" (Isaiah 50:6). And the deepest irony, the witnesses press, is that the One they mock as a false prophet had, that very night, prophesied this very scene — his condemnation, his abuse — now fulfilled to the letter by the men who jeer "Prophesy." The condemned Christ bears the spitting and the blows in silence, the Lord of glory submitting to the buffets of his creatures, that the Scriptures might be fulfilled and the ransom paid.
66:
"And as Peter was beneath in the palace, there cometh one of the maids of the high priest." The witnesses turn to Peter's denial and address the harmonising of the accounts. The Latin Augustine sets the order: "all the Evangelists do not speak in the same order; Luke first relates the temptation of Peter, then the injuries to the Lord; John begins the temptation, then puts in some things about the ill-treatment... Matthew and Mark first notice the injuries to Christ, then the temptation of Peter." On the denials, Augustine harmonises the maids: "this maid is not the same, but another, as Matthew says"; and "in the second denial he was addressed by two persons" — the maid and another. He concludes "Peter did not the second time deny him before the porch, but within the palace at the fire," Matthew and Mark omitting "for brevity" his return.

Bede draws a tender note from the maid: "what can be meant by his being first recognized by a woman, when men were more able to know him, if it be not that that sex might be seen to sin in the death of our Lord, and that sex be redeemed by his Passion?" And he presses the lesson: "by this denial of Peter we learn that not only he denies Christ who says he is not the Christ, but he also who, though he is a Christian, denies himself to be such."

So the witnesses make the verse the opening of Peter's fall and a case-study in Gospel harmony. The lesson the expositors draw, beyond reconciling the four accounts, is the searching reality of denial. Peter, who could not watch one hour and had followed afar off to warm himself among the enemies, is now recognized — and by a maidservant, the least and weakest of accusers. Augustine's careful harmonization, the witnesses note, shows the accounts not contradictory but complementary, each evangelist recording what another omits; the denials happened, in their full course, as all four together relate. Bede's reflections deepen the scene: that Peter was first challenged by a woman is read as a mystery of both sin and redemption; and his denial teaches that to deny Christ is not only to say "He is not the Christ" but, for a Christian, to deny that one belongs to him. The verse thus sets the stage for the threefold denial, in which the foremost apostle, who had boasted his loyalty above all, will fall at the word of a servant-girl — a warning, the witnesses press, of the weakness of the flesh that Christ had named, and a foreshadowing of the bitter repentance and restoration to come.
67:
"And when she saw Peter warming himself, she looked upon him, and said, And thou also wast with Jesus of Nazareth." The witnesses note the maid's pointed recognition and the scorn in her words. The grammatical-historical Ellicott reads the emphasis: the better manuscripts give the words "with an emphatic scorn, And thou also wast with the Nazarene, Jesus." Robertson notes Mark twice mentions Peter "warming himself" (vv.54, 67), as does John (18:18) — "he was twice beside the fire" — and that here it is "the Nazarene," where Matthew has "the Galilean," a sign that "a number were probably speaking, one saying one thing, another another." Gill notes she saw Peter "at the light which was in the midst of the hall."

The maid's challenge, the expositors agree, was made the easier by the very firelight Peter had sought for warmth, which shone on his face and marked him out. She "looked upon him" — fixed her gaze — and named him a follower of "Jesus of Nazareth," with a contempt the title carried.

So the witnesses make the verse the first assault upon Peter's courage. The lesson the expositors draw is the smallness of the occasion that overthrew the boldest disciple. It was no magistrate or armed officer that challenged him, but a servant-girl; and her recognition was made possible by his own imprudence — sitting at the fire, in the light, among the enemies of his Lord. The scorn in her words, the witnesses note (with Ellicott), is part of the trial: "thou also wast with the Nazarene, Jesus" — the despised title flung at the disciple of the despised Master. The firelight that Peter sought for comfort, Robertson observes, betrayed him; what he meant for concealment among the crowd exposed him to the maid's gaze. The verse thus shows how the disciple who had followed afar off and warmed himself among foes is brought, by the least of accusers and his own carelessness, to the brink of denial. The boast that he would die with Christ is about to be tested, not by death, but by the word of a girl by a fire; and the testing, the witnesses press, would prove how weak the flesh is when it has not watched and prayed, and how the proud confidence of Peter would crumble at the first breath of danger.
68:
"But he denied, saying, I know not, neither understand I what thou sayest. And he went out into the porch; and the cock crew." The witnesses note the first denial, its fuller form in Mark, and a textual question about the cock. The grammatical-historical Robertson observes that "this denial is fuller in Mark, briefest in John," and that "I neither know nor understand" can be read as a direct evasion. He explains the "porch" (proaulion, "only here in the New Testament") as "the vestibule to the court," and weighs the textual point: "and the cock crew" is "omitted by Aleph B L and the Sinaitic Syriac; it is genuine in verse 72... it is possible that because of verse 72 it crept into verse 68." He notes "Mark alone alludes to the cock crowing twice" (Mark 14:30).

Gill marks the denial's substance: Peter denied "that he was with Jesus, or a disciple of his," with the evasive "I know not, neither understand I what thou sayest" — a feigned ignorance, pretending not even to grasp the maid's meaning. Ellicott notes he "went out into the porch," edging away from the dangerous fire toward the exit.

So the witnesses make the verse the first of Peter's three denials. The lesson the expositors draw is the beginning of the fall and the warning of the cock. Peter, challenged by the maid, does not boldly confess nor even simply deny, but evades — "I know not, neither understand I what thou sayest" — feigning that he does not even understand the charge, the fuller and more deliberate denial that Mark records. And he moves away, out into the porch, seeking to escape further notice. At that moment, on the genuine reading, "the cock crew" — the first crowing, which (Mark alone records) should have recalled to Peter the Lord's warning that before the cock crew twice he would deny him thrice. The textual doubt, the witnesses note (with Robertson), does not touch the substance, for Mark's distinctive two-fold crowing is plain in v.72; whether the first crow is named here or only implied, the warning was sounding. The first denial, evasive and cowardly, is thus set against the crowing cock — the appointed sign Peter had been told to heed; and his failure to heed it, edging instead toward the porch and the next challenge, shows how far the boastful disciple had already fallen, and how the warning he had dismissed was even now beginning to be fulfilled.
69:
"And a maid saw him again, and began to say to them that stood by, This is one of them." The witnesses note the second challenge and a question of whose voice it was. The grammatical-historical Ellicott reads "the maid — i.e., the one that had pointed him out before," while Gill notes the versions differ — "either the same maid... or another, as in Matthew." Robertson explains that the charge was now made "to them that stood by," overheard by Peter: "This fellow is one of them," and notes that Augustine (v.66) had harmonised the second denial as addressed by two — the maid and another bystander (Luke).

The expositors mark the shift: the accusation is no longer to Peter directly but about him, to the bystanders — "This is one of them" — spreading the suspicion to the whole company at the fire. Peter, who had retreated to the porch, is recognized again and pointed out to all.

So the witnesses make the verse the renewal of the assault upon Peter and the widening of his danger. The lesson the expositors draw is the relentlessness of the temptation once the first denial is made. Having denied and edged away, Peter is not let alone; a maid sees him again and now publishes the charge to the standers-by — "This is one of them" — so that the whole group is alerted. The harmonization the fathers supply, the witnesses note (with Augustine and Robertson), shows that the second denial was provoked by more than one voice — the maid and another — the accusations multiplying as the suspicion grew. Peter is now caught in a rising tide: the first denial has not bought him safety but only drawn more eyes; the maid's words to the bystanders turn a private challenge into a public accusation. The lesson the expositors press is that one denial leads to another, and that the disciple who begins to deny his Lord finds the pressure not relieved but increased, until he is driven to deny again. The verse thus carries Peter from the first evasion toward the second, the net closing around him, and prepares for the deeper fall — the oath and the curse — by which, under mounting accusation, the boastful apostle would thrice disown the Master he had vowed to die for.
70:
"And he denied it again. And a little after, they that stood by said again to Peter, Surely thou art one of them: for thou art a Galilaean, and thy speech agreeth thereto." The witnesses note the second and the rising third challenge, and a textual point. The grammatical-historical Barnes cross-references the Galilean accent that marked Peter out (Acts 2:7), and Ellicott notes that the clause "and thy speech agreeth thereto" is "wanting in many of the best MSS., and may have been an interpretative addition, possibly made by St. Mark himself in a revised edition." Gill marks the second denial: Peter "denied it again, that he was one of the disciples of Jesus."

The third challenge, the expositors note, fastens on Peter's speech: the bystanders, hearing his Galilean accent, press the charge — "thou art a Galilaean" — for the dialect of Galilee betrayed him as a countryman of Jesus. What his words denied, his very manner of speaking confirmed.

So the witnesses make the verse the second denial and the mounting pressure toward the third. The lesson the expositors draw is the irony that Peter's own tongue betrayed him even as he used it to deny his Lord. He denies again that he is a disciple; but the bystanders, "a little after," renew the charge with new evidence — his Galilean speech, which "agreeth thereto." The very accent in which he framed his denials proclaimed him a Galilean, and so a follower of the Galilean Jesus. The detail, the witnesses note, is true to life: the dialect of Galilee was distinct, and Peter could not hide his origin even while denying his discipleship. The textual question about "thy speech agreeth thereto" — whether original or an early gloss, perhaps Mark's own — does not affect the substance, which all the Gospels report: it was his speech that gave him away. The mounting accusation, from the maid to the bystanders, from his presence to his very words, drives Peter toward the climax of his fall; for the more he denies, the more the evidence accumulates against him, until, cornered and desperate, he will resort in the next verse to oaths and curses to make his denial believed — the lowest depth of the apostle's failure.
71:
"But he began to curse and to swear, saying, I know not this man of whom ye speak." The witnesses note the depth of Peter's third denial. The grammatical-historical Robertson examines "curse" (anathematizein): "our word — finally distinguished as anathema for curse; Deissmann has found examples at Megara of anathema in the sense of curse." He notes Matthew has a related word "which has the notion of calling down curses on one's self if the thing is not true." Gill explains: Peter began "to wish the most dreadful things upon himself, and to swear by the living God."

The expositors mark the climax of the fall. Where the first denial was an evasion and the second a flat denial, the third is reinforced with cursing and swearing — Peter calling down curses upon himself and taking oaths to make his denial credible, declaring "I know not this man" of the very Lord he had confessed and vowed to die with.

So the witnesses make the verse the nadir of Peter's denial. The lesson the expositors draw is how far a disciple may fall when, having begun to deny, he is driven by fear and the rising pressure to deny more and more vehemently. Peter, cornered by the accumulating evidence — his presence, his being one of them, his Galilean speech — now seeks to silence all suspicion by the strongest means: he curses and swears, invoking damnation on himself if he is lying, to prove that he knows not "this man." The phrase "this man," the witnesses note, is itself the measure of his disowning: the Lord whom he had called the Christ, the Son of the living God, he now refers to as a stranger — "this man of whom ye speak" — as though he had never known him. The cursing and swearing, Robertson observes, carry the notion of calling down curses on himself; the apostle stakes his very soul on the lie. This is the third and deepest denial, the boastful Peter who had drawn the sword now grovelling before a serving-maid and bystanders, denying his Master with oaths. And it is the lowest point — for at this very moment, the next verse records, the cock crew the second time, and the Lord's word came home, breaking Peter's heart in the bitter tears of repentance.
72:
"And the second time the cock crew. And Peter called to mind the word that Jesus said unto him, Before the cock crow twice, thou shalt deny me thrice. And when he thought thereon, he wept." The witnesses note the second crowing, the remembrance, and the obscure phrase for Peter's weeping.
