The grammatical-historical Gill marks the time: "the second time the cock crew, immediately... which was about three of the clock in the morning, and is what is properly called the cock crowing." Mark alone, the witnesses note (with Robertson on v.68), records the cock crowing twice, fulfilling exactly the Lord's word — "before the cock crow twice, thou shalt deny me thrice."

On "when he thought thereon, he wept," the commentators weigh the difficult Greek epibalōn. Clarke: it "comes nearest to the original — he fell a weeping; others think it means the wrapping of his head in his garment through shame and anguish; others that it refers to the violence with which he left the place." Ellicott surveys the same: "literally throwing at, or on; the English version assumes casting his mind or thoughts... probably right," though some read "casting himself down, he wept."

So the witnesses make the verse the turning of Peter's denial into repentance. The lesson the expositors draw is the power of remembered grace to break a fallen heart. At the second crowing of the cock — the precise sign the Lord had foretold — Peter "called to mind the word that Jesus said," and the memory of Christ's warning, and of his own boast, pierced him; "and when he thought thereon, he wept." The exactness of the fulfilment, the witnesses note, is striking: Mark's distinctive twofold crowing matches the prediction to the letter, so that the very sound that marked Peter's failure was the sign that recalled him. The obscure word for his weeping — whether he "fell a weeping," covered his head in shame, or hurried from the place in anguish — the witnesses leave, with the commentators, in its uncertainty; but its sense is plain: Peter, struck by remembrance, broke down in bitter tears. The contrast with Judas, the witnesses press (across this section), is the great lesson: both betrayed their Lord, but Judas despaired and perished, while Peter, brought to himself by the crowing cock and the remembered word, "wept" — the tears of a true repentance that would issue, after the resurrection, in his restoration. The denial that was Peter's deepest fall becomes, through grace, the occasion of his recovery; and the weeping apostle stands as the perpetual emblem of the disciple who falls grievously, yet, recalled by the word of Christ, finds his way back through penitent tears.
1:
On Mark 14:1 — “After two days was the feast of the passover, and of unleavened bread: and the chief priests… sought how they might take him by craft, and put him to death” — the witnesses open the Passion week and weigh the conspiracy.

The Latin fathers read the Passover typologically. Bede explains that Pascha — Hebrew phase — comes “not from Passion… but from passing over,” the destroyer passing the blood-marked doors (Exodus 12:13); Pseudo-Jerome heaps up figures of the slain Lamb — the scarlet thread, the red heifer (Numbers 19:2) — as the Evangelist prepares to tell of Christ’s death. The Eastern Theophylact adds that “Christ Himself had determined… the day of His Passion; for He wished to be crucified on the Passover, because He was the true Passover.”

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the chronology. Robertson dates this “Tuesday evening as we count time,” reconciling Mark and Matthew, and noting John’s items “really in harmony.” Barnes glosses “by craft” as “by some secret plan that would secure possession of him without exciting the opposition of the people.”

So the witnesses set the scene of the Passion in its double light. While the rulers plot in secret to seize Jesus “by craft” and avoid a festival tumult, the deeper truth is that the day is not theirs to choose: the true Paschal Lamb has appointed his own hour, willing to be offered at the very feast that for centuries had foreshadowed him. The Puritan Henry, looking ahead to the supper at Bethany, marks that Christ had friends as well as enemies in those days — some who “never thought they could do enough for him” — so that the chapter opens with malice and devotion side by side, the plot of the priests answered by the love that would anoint him for his burial.

Pseudo-Jerome would have the believer enter the story as into the Passover itself — “let us now sprinkle our book… with blood, and put the scarlet thread around the house of our prayers” (Genesis 38:30) — that we may keep the true Passover with the slain Lamb. The witnesses thus read the chapter’s opening as no mere chronicle of a plot but the threshold of redemption. The priests reckon by craft and fear; the Evangelist reckons by the calendar of grace, “after two days… the passover.” And over the rulers’ scheming stands the quiet sovereignty of the Lamb who chose the feast for his offering — so that the same day they meant for secret murder God had appointed, from of old, for the redemption of the world.
2:
On Mark 14:2 — “But they said, Not on the feast day, lest there be an uproar of the people” — the witnesses note how the rulers’ calculation was overruled by a higher purpose.

The grammatical-historical Robertson sets the change of plan in its history: “they had first planned to kill him at the feast” (John 11:57), “but the Triumphal Entry and great Tuesday debate… in the temple had made them decide to wait till after the feast was over,” for “it was plain that Jesus had too large and powerful a following.” The Reformed Gill agrees: with the great concourse of pilgrims gathering, “they did not choose to seize him… at that time” for fear of the crowd.

The verse, brief as it is, turns on the irony the whole Passion narrative will unfold. The priests, masters of their own plans as they thought, resolved to delay until the feast was past and the dangerous crowds dispersed; yet the event fell out precisely on the feast day, at the very hour the paschal lambs were slain. Their caution was real, their power great, their reasoning shrewd — and all of it was overruled.

So the witnesses read in the rulers’ “not on the feast day” a quiet testimony to the providence of God. Men proposed a later, safer date; God had fixed this one. The unexpected treachery of Judas, breaking in upon their delay, would hurry the deed forward against their own decision, so that the true Passover was sacrificed on the great day of the paschal solemnity. The verse thus stands as a small monument to a truth the Evangelists love to display: that the wrath of men, plot as it will, serves in the end the counsel it means to thwart, and the death of Christ came neither too soon nor too late, but at the appointed time.

Robertson refers the reader to the seizure itself (Matthew 26:47), where the very thing the council resolved to avoid — a taking during the feast — came to pass through Judas’s unlooked-for offer. So the witnesses read the verse as a hinge between man’s plan and God’s. The rulers were not fools; their caution was sound statecraft, and had the matter rested with them the death of Jesus might have waited. But it did not rest with them. The treachery of a disciple would override their prudence and hurry the deed to the very day they had ruled out — and in that overruling the witnesses see, not the triumph of malice, but the hand of God bringing the true Passover to its appointed hour, that the Scriptures might be fulfilled.
3:
On Mark 14:3 — the supper at Bethany, where a woman “brake the box” of “very precious” spikenard and poured it on Jesus’ head — the witnesses give much labour to the woman’s identity and the meaning of her gift.

The great patristic debate is whether the Gospels speak of one anointing woman or several. Theophylact counts “two women and not one” — or, on closer view, three, John’s Mary of Bethany being distinct from Luke’s “sinner.” Augustine, by contrast, holds the sinner of Luke and the Mary of John to be the same woman who “did this twice” (John 11:2). Bede and Pseudo-Jerome read the scene allegorically: Bethany is “the house of obedience,” Simon (once a leper, now healed) “the world… afterwards converted,” the woman “the faith of the Church” whose spikenard is “faithful and precious.” Origen presses the lesson that the ointment was rightly poured on Christ, “a good work” to stir us all to fill his head with sweet odours.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe the gift. Barnes explains the “ointment” was a liquid perfume of Indian spikenard; Robertson notes the rare word pistic (genuine) nard, used elsewhere only in John 12:3, and (with the discussion at Matthew 26:6) holds this “a different incident from that recorded in” Luke 7:36. Lightfoot and Clarke debate the alabaster flask and the costly balsam.

So the witnesses honour the act even as they dispute the woman’s name. Whatever the harmonising, the deed is one: a lavish, “wasteful” love that broke an alabaster vessel of great price over the head of the Lord, days before his death. The fathers find in it the Church’s faith pouring out its best on Christ; the expositors find in it a singular devotion that, as the next verses show, the Lord himself defended and immortalised. Set against the priests’ plotting and Judas’s greed, the broken box at Bethany stands as love’s extravagance answering the world’s calculation.

Lightfoot enters the lore of the perfume — the costly “balanus” and “myrobalanum” of the ancient writers — to show how great a treasure was here poured out; Origen turns it to exhortation, that the praise of this “good work” should “excite all of us” to anoint the head of the Lord with the sweet ointment of holy deeds. So the witnesses, for all their dispute over the woman’s name, are one in the lesson. Whether she be Mary of Bethany or another, whether this be the act of John’s Gospel or a second like it, the thing that matters is the heart that broke the box: a love that counted nothing too precious for Christ, and that the Lord himself would shield from every murmur and crown with an everlasting memorial.
4:
On Mark 14:4 — “And there were some that had indignation within themselves, and said, Why was this waste of the ointment made?” — the witnesses trace the murmur to its source.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott marks “St. Mark’s limitation of the murmurers to ‘some,’ as an intermediate stage between St. Matthew’s ‘the disciples’ and St. John’s naming ‘Judas.’” The Reformed Gill notes that the Syriac reads “some of the disciples,” and that the indignation was kindled “particularly” by Judas, who incited the rest. The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley agrees: “some had indignation, being incited thereto by Judas,” and “said” their complaint “probably to the women.”

The verse exposes how a complaint that wears the dress of conscience may spring from a corrupt heart. To eyes that could not love, the breaking of the box was “waste”; the very lavishness that made it precious to Christ made it offensive to them. And the murmur, once voiced by one covetous soul, spread to others who took it up without discerning its source.

So the witnesses set Mary’s devotion against the disciples’ — or rather Judas’s — calculation, and observe how easily honest men are drawn into a plausible grumbling. The word “waste” (literally “perdition”) is the world’s perpetual verdict on whatever is poured out wholly upon God: what love counts well spent, a grudging spirit counts thrown away. The Evangelist’s careful “some” both shields the body of the disciples and points, as John will make plain, to the one whose greed first gave the murmur a voice — a small but telling instance of how the betrayer’s heart was already turned against the generosity of grace.

So the witnesses weigh the murmur and find it wanting. Wesley notes the complaint was uttered “probably to the women” — a public shaming of the giver — and that, though Judas struck the first note, “several of them were angry.” The verse thus warns how a covetous heart can dress its grievance in the language of charity, and how quickly a plausible objection spreads from one to many. What love offers freely, calculation calls “waste”; and the same deed that the Lord receives as honour, the grudging spirit resents as loss. The Evangelist sets the scene that we may judge ourselves — whether we too, under fair pretexts of prudence or pity, would stint the costliest devotion that is poured upon Christ.
5:
On Mark 14:5 — “For it might have been sold for more than three hundred pence, and have been given to the poor. And they murmured against her” — the witnesses reckon the sum and the snorting indignation.

The grammatical-historical commentators price the gift. Gill puts the three hundred pence at “nine pounds, seven shillings, and sixpence”; Barnes at about forty dollars (cf. Matthew 18:28); Ellicott notes the specific sum is one of “the few points common to St. Mark and St. John” (John 12:5). Lightfoot cites Pliny on the known market prices of nard. Robertson fastens on the striking verb “they murmured against her” (enebrimōnto), “used of the snorting of horses” — a detail perhaps “from Peter’s memory of Judas’ objection,” whose name John 12:4 preserves.

The verse lays the murmur bare in its full meanness. A year’s wages, they reckon, has been “wasted” in a moment; and the plea “given to the poor” lends the complaint a charitable colour it does not deserve. The snorting indignation against the woman — for it is against her that they turn, not against any real loss — betrays a heart that resents devotion it cannot share.

So the witnesses gather the scene: the costliest gift, the basest grudging, and a Lord about to answer for the silent woman. The precise three hundred pence, the calculating tongue, the animal snort of resentment — all are set down by the Evangelist that we may feel the contrast he intends. Where Mary measures nothing and gives all, the murmurers measure everything and give nothing; and the very arithmetic by which they condemn her — so much money, so many poor — only exposes how far a religion of mere reckoning stands from the love that the Lord receives and rewards.

Lightfoot, citing Pliny, sets down the known prices of such perfumes — nard reckoned by weight at great cost — to show the gift was no trifle but a true treasure; and Robertson notes that the verb “murmured” (used in the Greek Old Testament of deep, angry emotion) marks not a mild demur but a fierce resentment. So the witnesses let the contrast stand at its sharpest. The murmurers reckon to the last penny what might have been gained; Mary reckons nothing, and gives all. Their charity is loud and costs them nothing; her love is silent and costs her everything. And the Lord, who reads both hearts, will in the next breath turn their arithmetic to shame and set her unmeasured gift among the things that shall be told wherever the gospel is preached.
6:
On Mark 14:6 — “And Jesus said, Let her alone; why trouble ye her? she hath wrought a good work on me” — the witnesses hear the Lord rise to the silent woman’s defence.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott marks that the “good” work is “good in the sense of ‘noble,’ as implying the higher form of goodness” — a usage here “peculiar to St. Mark.” The Reformed Gill notes that Jesus, “knowing the secret indignation of some of his disciples, and their private murmuring… and their continual teasings of her,” said, “Let the woman alone, cease to chide and reprove her for what she has done.”

The verse turns the whole scene about. What the murmurers called “waste” the Lord calls a “good” — indeed a noble — “work,” and what they would correct in her he forbids them to touch. “Let her alone” is at once a shield over Mary and a rebuke to her critics: she has done nothing that needs defending before him, for he himself is her defence.

So the witnesses find in Christ’s few words the vindication of love’s extravagance. He does not weigh the ointment against the poor, nor measure the deed by its price; he receives it as a thing wrought “on me,” an honour rendered to his person in the very shadow of his death. The disciples saw an account-book; the Lord saw a heart. And in bidding them “trouble her” no more, he teaches that those who pour themselves out upon Christ are answerable not to the grumbling of men but to the Master who reads and approves the gift — a comfort to every believer whose costliest devotion the world has counted folly.

So the witnesses gather the Lord’s defence into a single principle: that devotion to Christ is answerable to Christ alone. He does not enter into the disciples’ calculation, nor concede that the poor have been wronged; he simply receives the deed as “a good work on me” and bids the murmurers cease. Ellicott’s note that the word means “noble” lifts the act above mere kindness to a thing of moral beauty. The verse thus comforts every believer whose costliest offering the world has counted folly: the Master who knows the secret indignation of the critics knows also the love of the giver, and against all the grumbling of men he stands as the giver’s advocate, naming the despised gift a noble work wrought upon himself.
7:
On Mark 14:7 — “For ye have the poor with you always… but me ye have not always” — the witnesses balance the perpetual duty of charity against the unrepeatable hour of honouring Christ.

The grammatical-historical commentators ground the saying in the Law. Barnes and Lightfoot cite Deuteronomy 15:11 — “the poor shall never cease out of the land” — Lightfoot adding the rabbinic admission “that there shall be poor men even in the days of the Messias.” Ellicott marks that Mark alone preserves the added word, “Whensoever ye will ye may do them good”: the opportunity of charity is constant, while the bodily presence of the Lord is passing.

The verse, the witnesses insist, is no disparagement of the poor but a setting of duties in their order. Christ does not forbid almsgiving; he commands it — “whensoever ye will” — but he marks that there was a thing to be done that night which could be done at no other: the anointing of his body against its burial. The poor would remain; he would not.

So the witnesses read a lesson against a religion that would always defer the worship of Christ to the service of men, as if the two were rivals. There is a time for the poor, which is all the time; and there was a time for Mary’s ointment, which was then or never. The same love that pours itself on Christ will not lack occasion to relieve his poor; but the murmurers, who cared in truth for neither, used the poor only to grudge the Lord his honour. The verse thus orders, without opposing, the two great works of love — and reserves to Christ a worship that is his due even above the claims of charity.

Lightfoot adds the rabbinic saying of Samuel, that “there is no difference between this world and the days of the Messias” save in the affliction of the nations — a Jew confessing “that there shall be poor men even in the days of the Messias” (Deuteronomy 15:11). So the witnesses guard the verse from abuse on either side. It does not license neglect of the poor, whom we are to relieve “whensoever” we will; nor does it make charity a rival to worship. It orders the two: the poor are a standing trust, always with us; the bodily presence of Christ, to be honoured in that hour, would soon be gone. The love that pours itself on the Lord and the love that stoops to the poor are one love, rightly timed — and only a false heart pits the second against the first to excuse its grudging of both.
8:
On Mark 14:8 — “She hath done what she could: she is come aforehand to anoint my body to the burying” — the witnesses find in Mary an understanding the rest had not.

The grammatical-historical Robertson renders the Greek bluntly — “what she had she did” — and draws the point Mark alone records: “Mary could not comprehend the Lord’s death, but she at least showed her sympathy… and some understanding of the coming tragedy, a thing that not one of her critics had done.” Clarke explains “to the burying” as “in reference to its embalmment,” the bodies of the honoured being “wrapped up in aromatics” (cf. Matthew 26:12). Barnes adds that her deed was “a sufficient argument against there being any real waste, that it was done for the honour of Christ.”

The Lord’s praise, “she hath done what she could,” is among the gentlest in the Gospels. He does not say she did all that might be done, nor that she fully grasped its meaning; he says she did what lay in her power and her love, and that is enough. Her ointment, poured in devotion, he receives as an anticipated embalming — love’s unwitting preparation for the grave so near.

So the witnesses set Mary’s instinct above the apostles’ blindness. While the Twelve dreamed of a kingdom and could not hear his repeated word of death, this woman’s heart, if not her understanding, ran ahead to the tomb. Christ honours not the measure of her knowledge but the truth of her love; “what she could” she did, and he asks no more of any. The verse thus comforts the humblest service offered in faith: the Lord does not despise the little that is all we have, but accepts the gift according to the heart that brings it, and reads in it more than the giver knew.

Robertson renders the Greek with care — “she took beforehand to anoint my body for the burying” — the same word used of the spices at the entombment (John 19:40); and Clarke notes the bodies of the honoured were “wrapped up in aromatics” against decay. So the witnesses find in Mary a faith that outran the apostles’. She could not have explained the cross, yet her love moved toward it; while the Twelve, deaf to his repeated word of death, dreamed of thrones, this woman’s heart prepared his grave. The Lord’s gentle “she hath done what she could” sets the measure of all acceptable service: not the greatness of the gift nor the fulness of the understanding, but the truth of the love that gives what it has to Christ.
9:
On Mark 14:9 — “Wheresoever this gospel shall be preached throughout the whole world, this also that she hath done shall be spoken of for a memorial of her” — the witnesses hear a tribute that is also a prophecy.

The grammatical-historical Robertson draws the contrast that the centuries have proved: “there are many mausoleums that crumble to decay. But this monument to Jesus fills the whole world still with its fragrance” — “what a hint there is here for those who wish to leave permanent memorials.” Clarke refers the reader to the fuller note at Matthew 26:13. The Reformed Gill prefaces the saying with Christ’s solemn “Verily,” bidding us “assure” ourselves of its truth.

The promise is doubly remarkable. It assumes, at the very hour of his rejection and approaching death, that the gospel shall be preached “throughout the whole world” — a foresight, the commentators note, “not less than divine”; and it weds to that worldwide proclamation the memory of one woman’s act of love. Where others saw only waste, the Lord saw a deed worthy to travel with his gospel to the ends of the earth.

So the witnesses find in the verse both honour and forecast. The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem, dwelling on the gospel thus to be preached, turns to the eternal life it offers by faith. And the standing lesson is plain: the memorial Christ raises endures when marble fails. Mary sought no monument; she broke her box in silence and bore the murmurs of the room. Yet her Lord decreed that her name should be carried wherever his cross is told — a pledge that nothing poured out upon Christ is ever lost, and that the love the world forgets, heaven keeps and the gospel itself perpetuates.

The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem, taking up the gospel that should be preached in all the world, points to the eternal life it offers by faith — “He that believeth on the Son hath everlasting life” (John 6:47). So the witnesses read the verse as honour and prophecy in one. That the gospel would reach “the whole world” was, at that hour of rejection, a forecast “not less than divine”; and that one woman’s silent deed should travel with it is the Lord’s own seal upon the worth of love. Robertson’s contrast abides: marble monuments crumble, but this memorial “fills the whole world still with its fragrance.” Nothing poured out upon Christ is lost; what the murmurers called waste, heaven has made imperishable, carried wherever the cross is told.
10:
On Mark 14:10 — “And Judas Iscariot, one of the twelve, went unto the chief priests, to betray him unto them” — the witnesses mark the dark contrast with the love just shown.

The Latin and Eastern fathers probe the betrayer. Bede notes that “the unhappy Judas wishes to compensate… the loss which he thought he had made by the pouring out of the ointment,” and that “he went out” shows he was “not invited… nor bound by any necessity, but entered upon this design from the spontaneous wickedness of his own mind.” Pseudo-Jerome: he was “one of the twelve in number, not in merit, one in body, not in soul,” who went after “Satan entered into him.” Chrysostom marks the Evangelist’s restraint — he names Judas’s town to distinguish him, but does not call him “the traitor,” teaching us “to be guiltless of detraction.”

The grammatical-historical Robertson weighs the article, “the one of the twelve,” calling attention to the horror that “he was the one of the twelve who did this deed.” The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley dates it: “immediately after this reproof, having anger now added to his covetousness.”

So the witnesses set the bargain hard upon the anointing, that the blackness of the one may be felt against the brightness of the other. While a woman breaks a costly box upon the Lord, a chosen apostle goes out to sell him; while love pours out its all, greed seeks its price. The fathers are careful to lodge the guilt where it belongs — in Judas’s own free and spontaneous will, not in any compulsion of fate — and to mark that he was of the Twelve in name only. The verse thus opens the tragedy of the betrayal with a warning that nearness to Christ is no safety, and that the same supper which drew out Mary’s devotion hardened Judas’s heart to its ruin.

Pseudo-Jerome marks the dark fellowship of like with like: Judas “went to the chief priests… and Satan entered into him,” for “every living thing unites with what is like itself” — he promises to betray, as his master the devil had said, “all this power I will give thee” (Luke 4:6). Chrysostom marks that the Evangelist distinguishes this Judas from the faithful Jude, yet withholds the name “traitor,” teaching us to shun detraction. So the witnesses set the bargain against the anointing in deliberate contrast: love’s extravagance and greed’s calculation, the broken box and the bargained price. And they lodge the guilt where it belongs — in a will freely given over to evil — warning that a place among the Twelve is no safety, and that the heart unsurrendered may sit at Christ’s table and go out to sell him.
11:
On Mark 14:11 — “And when they heard it, they were glad, and promised to give him money. And he sought how he might conveniently betray him” — the witnesses note the priests’ unholy gladness.

The grammatical-historical Robertson observes that “the rabbis looked on the treachery of Judas as a veritable dispensation of Providence amply justifying their plots”; the “conveniently” (eukairōs) is “the whole point of the offer” — Judas claimed to know Jesus’s habits well enough to take him “in the absence of the multitude” (Luke 22:6). Ellicott notes Mark, like Luke, records the priests’ gladness but, unlike Matthew, “does not name the specific sum.” The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke recoils: “the joy that arises from the opportunity of murdering an innocent person must be completely infernal.”

The verse fastens on the gladness. The rulers, who had been perplexed how to seize Jesus without a tumult, now hear an offer from within his own circle and rejoice — not at any good, but at the chance of a secret, convenient murder. Their joy is the measure of their guilt.

So the witnesses gather the meeting of the two evils: the priests’ eager welcome and the disciple’s patient scheming. Money is promised; Judas sets himself to watch for the opportune moment, away from the crowds that loved his Master. Barnes points to the warnings of Scripture against such confederacies of greed and blood (Proverbs 1:10–16). The verse stands as a grim portrait of sin finding its allies: the covetous apostle and the murderous council, each glad of the other, conspiring to do quietly what neither dared do openly — and all of it, the Evangelists will show, serving the very purpose of God they meant to thwart.

Barnes sets the priests’ gladness beside the Scripture’s portraits of those who “sell themselves to work evil” (1 Kings 21:20; Proverbs 1:10–16); Robertson notes that “conveniently” was the whole value of Judas’s offer — he could deliver his Master quietly, “in the absence of the multitude” (Luke 22:6). So the witnesses read the meeting of two evils that needed each other: the council that dared not act for fear of the crowd, and the disciple who knew how to act in secret. Money is promised; the watch for an opportunity begins. The verse holds up the grim spectacle of sin glad of its allies — and, beneath it, the providence that would turn even this confederacy of greed and blood to the accomplishment of the redemption it sought to prevent.
12:
On Mark 14:12 — “And the first day of unleavened bread, when they killed the passover, his disciples said… Where wilt thou that we go and prepare?” — the witnesses fix the day and the contrast with Judas’s plotting.

The Latin and Eastern fathers read the transition from old to new. Bede explains the day as “the fourteenth… when they throw aside leaven” and slay the lamb at even, on which night Christ “committed to His disciples the Mysteries of His Body” — for “Christ our Passover is sacrificed for us” (1 Corinthians 5:7). Chrysostom marks that the disciples prepared “not our Passover, but… that of the Jews” — yet Christ “Himself became our Passover.” Theophylact infers from “where wilt thou that we go?” that “Christ had no dwelling-place,” and that, sending two to an unknown host, he showed he might, had he willed, have avoided his Passion.

The grammatical-historical commentators handle the chronology and the sign. Robertson notes the imperfect “when they sacrificed the passover” (the lamb slain toward evening, Exodus 12:6, preparations on the fourteenth); Lightfoot enters the disputed harmony of the day. Barnes marks that the man bearing a pitcher “could have been known only by the infinite knowledge of Christ.”

So the witnesses set the quiet obedience of the Eleven against the secret treachery of the one. While Judas plots, the rest are busy about the Passover, asking only where to prepare it. The fathers find in the old feast, kept this last time, the very threshold of the new: that night the typical lamb gives way to the true, and the supper of remembrance to the Supper of his Body and Blood. The Puritan Henry draws the practical word — that “no apprehension of trouble… should put us out of frame” for holy ordinances; the Lord, with the cross full in view, keeps the feast, and so teaches his people to draw near to God in the very shadow of their sorrows.

Augustine harmonises the accounts of the sign — “Mark says a pitcher, Luke a two-handed vessel; one points out the kind of vessel, the other the mode of carrying it; both… mean the same truth.” So the witnesses set the disciples’ busy obedience against Judas’s secret plotting, and find in the last Passover the threshold of the first Supper. That night, kept according to the old law, the typical lamb gives way to the true; “Christ our Passover is sacrificed for us” (1 Corinthians 5:7). And Henry’s practical word stands: with the cross full in view, the Lord still keeps the feast — teaching that no shadow of coming trouble should put his people out of frame for the ordinances in which he gives himself to them.
13:
On Mark 14:13 — “And he sendeth forth two of his disciples, and saith… there shall meet you a man bearing a pitcher of water: follow him” — the witnesses marvel at the Lord’s foreknowledge.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the detail peculiar to Mark and Luke. Ellicott and Robertson observe that “the number is given by St. Mark; the names, Peter and John, by St. Luke” (Luke 22:8), and that “the sign of the pitcher of water” is common to both but absent from Matthew. Clarke draws the wider lesson: “how correct is the foreknowledge of Jesus Christ! Even the minutest circumstances are comprehended by it” — and “an honest employment, howsoever mean,” such as a man carrying water, “is worthy the attention of God.”

The sign was apt and exact. A man bearing a water-pitcher — commonly a woman’s task — would stand out, an unmistakable token; and the two disciples had only to follow him to the appointed house. The Lord who could not be taken “by craft” until his hour orders the smallest particulars of his last Passover with sovereign precision.

So the witnesses find in the errand a double comfort. First, Wesley notes, it was “highly seasonable” to give the disciples “this additional proof both of his knowing all things, and of his influence over the minds of men,” on the very eve of his sufferings, that their faith might be steadied. Second, Clarke’s reflection abides: the God who marks a poor man’s steps with a pitcher of water is mindful of the lowliest, honest labour, and weaves even such humble service into his purposes. The verse, slight in itself, shows the Master moving toward the cross not as a victim of circumstance but as the Lord of all circumstances, ordering each step of the way.

Clarke notes that the man bore home “the water which was to be used for baking the unleavened bread,” an ordinary errand made the vehicle of a sovereign sign. So the witnesses find in the brief direction a double comfort on the eve of the Passion. It proves, Wesley says, Christ’s “knowing all things, and… his influence over the minds of men,” steadying the disciples’ faith against the dark hours at hand; and it shows, Clarke adds, that “an honest employment, howsoever mean,” is “an object of the merciful regards of the Most High.” The God who marks a poor man’s steps with a water-jar and weaves them into the preparing of his Son’s last supper is the Lord of the least as of the greatest, ordering every circumstance toward his purpose.
14:
On Mark 14:14 — “say ye to the goodman of the house, The Master saith, Where is the guestchamber, where I shall eat the passover with my disciples?” — the witnesses note the quiet authority of the request.

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the words. Barnes explains “the goodman of the house” as simply “the master of the house,” the term saying nothing of his character; Robertson notes the late word oikodespotēs and that katalyma, “guest-chamber,” is the same word rendered “inn” in Luke 2:7. Ellicott observes that the better manuscripts read “Where is my guest-chamber,” a form that “implies discipleship on the part of the owner… even more than” Matthew’s. Clarke adds the Eastern custom of a “strangers’ room” set apart for guests.

The message carries a calm lordship. “The Master saith” — the host will know who is meant; and the very phrase “my guest-chamber” suggests one who already owns Jesus as Lord and has, perhaps, held the room ready. Christ asks, but as one who has only to ask.

So the witnesses see in this brief errand the union of humility and authority that marks the whole approach to the Passion. The Lord who has “no dwelling-place” of his own borrows an upper room; yet he secures it not by chance but by a word that the householder will at once obey. The hidden disciple, ready with his chamber, stands among those secret friends who served Christ in Jerusalem when open confession was perilous — a reminder that the Lord has more followers than the crowds know, and that he provides, by the quiet devotion of such, the very place where he will institute the feast of his Body and Blood.

Robertson notes the late word for “goodman” (oikodespotēs), found in the papyri, and that the “guest-chamber” is the very word of the manger-narrative — the “inn” that had no room (Luke 2:7). So the witnesses hear in the message a quiet lordship. “The Master saith” assumes the host will know and obey; the reading “my guest-chamber” implies a man already a disciple, who had perhaps kept the room ready. The Lord who owns no house of his own commands an upper room with a word, providing by the secret devotion of an unnamed friend the very place where he will give the Supper. Humility and authority meet: he borrows, yet as one who has only to ask, and the hidden disciple’s readiness becomes a part of the gospel’s story.
15:
On Mark 14:15 — “And he will shew you a large upper room furnished and prepared: there make ready for us” — the witnesses picture the chamber of the Last Supper.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe the room. Barnes: “a large upper room… furnished and prepared” means “literally, spread and ready — spread with a carpet, or with couches, such as were used in eating.” Robertson notes anagaion, “anything above ground… upstairs,” and that “Jesus wishes to observe this last feast with his disciples alone, not with others.” Ellicott explains the two words: the first that it was “not a bare, empty chamber, but set out with cushions or divans,” the second that it was “specially arranged for the Paschal Supper of that evening.” Clarke notes the Jews no longer ate the Passover standing, as their fathers, but reclining.

The detail is homely and exact. The room was upstairs, large enough for the thirteen, already spread with the couches and arranged for the feast — everything in readiness, so that the two disciples had only to procure the lamb and the bread. The Lord’s foreknowledge, shown in the man with the pitcher, reaches here too: the place is found exactly as he said.

So the witnesses set the stage for the great institution with quiet care. In this borrowed upper room, furnished by an unnamed friend, Christ would keep the last Passover and give the first Supper; here the old covenant meal would pass into the new. The very ordinariness of the preparation — a carpeted chamber, couches spread, a meal made ready — frames the extraordinary thing about to be done in it. The God who orders pitchers and guest-rooms is the same who will, that night, take bread and wine and make them the abiding memorial of his death until he come.

Robertson notes the word for the room (anagaion, an upstairs chamber, with a classical example in Xenophon) and the perfect participle “furnished” — a settled state of readiness. So the witnesses frame the great institution within homely particulars: a carpeted upper room, couches spread, all made ready by an unseen hand, that the two disciples had only to bring the lamb and the bread. The very ordinariness sets off the wonder to be done there. In this borrowed chamber the old Passover would be eaten for the last time and the new Supper given for the first; here the meal of remembrance for the Exodus would pass into the meal of remembrance for the cross. The God who orders pitchers and guest-rooms orders also the place where he will leave his people the abiding memorial of his death.
16:
On Mark 14:16 — “And his disciples went forth, and came into the city, and found as he had said unto them: and they made ready the passover” — the witnesses mark the exact fulfilment and the disciples’ obedience.

The voices here are brief. The Reformed Gill identifies the two as Peter and John, who “set out from Bethany to Jerusalem directly” and found all as Christ foretold. The grammatical-historical Barnes points to the larger truth the verse illustrates — that the Lord’s word does not fail (John 16:4): they “found as he had said.”

The verse is a small monument to two things at once: the truth of Christ’s prediction and the simplicity of the disciples’ obedience. They were sent to an unknown house on the strength of a strange sign, with no explanation; and they went, and found, and made ready. The sign met them exactly as promised — the man, the house, the room — confirming once more, on the eve of the Passion, that he who was about to be betrayed and slain was no helpless victim but the Lord who sees the end from the beginning.

So the witnesses read in the disciples’ ready compliance a pattern of faith. They did not stay to question how their Master could know what awaited them in the city; they simply did as he said, and the doing brought them to the very threshold of the greatest evening of their lives. The verse joins the prophet’s accuracy to the servant’s trust: where Christ commands, however dark or strange the errand, the obedient find that his word goes before them and makes their way plain, and that nothing he foretells of his own approaching sufferings is less sure than this small sign so exactly fulfilled.

So the witnesses make the verse a small monument to two truths: the certainty of Christ’s word and the simplicity of obedient faith. Sent to an unknown house on the strength of a strange sign, with no reasons given, the two disciples went and “found as he had said” (John 16:4). Every particular met them as foretold — the man, the house, the furnished room — a further proof, on the eve of the Passion, that he who was about to be betrayed and slain was no helpless victim but the Lord who sees the end from the beginning. And their ready compliance, asking nothing but doing all, is the pattern of the faith that takes Christ at his word: it does not wait to understand before it obeys, and finds, in obeying, that his word has gone before to make the way plain.
17:
On Mark 14:17 — “And in the evening he cometh with the twelve” — the witnesses mark the gathering of the company, the traitor among them.

The Latin and Eastern fathers read the Lord’s dealing with Judas. Bede: having foretold his Passion, Christ “prophesied also of the traitor, in order to give him room for repentance, that… he might repent.” Chrysostom marks the balance — Jesus “did not proclaim him openly to all, lest He should make him the more shameless; at the same time He did not altogether keep it silent, lest… he should boldly hasten to betray Him.” Theophylact takes up the old question how they could recline when the Law bade them eat the Passover standing, and answers that “they had first fulfilled the legal Passover, and had reclined” when Christ began his own. Pseudo-Jerome hears in the disciples’ “Is it I?” the harmony of a harp, all the strings answering true save the one “unstrung” by sin.

The grammatical-historical Robertson reads the dramatic present “he cometh,” and takes Jesus to be keeping “the regular passover meal at the regular time and hour, at 6 P.M.” The Reformed Gill pictures the company assembling at evening — the nine, with Judas, and Peter and John who had prepared the feast.

So the witnesses set the scene of the supper with its undercurrent of sorrow. The Twelve recline; the Lord, knowing all, comes to keep the feast with them; and the betrayer sits among the rest, not yet unmasked. The fathers dwell on Christ’s mingled mercy and warning toward Judas — enough said to give him room to turn, not so much as to shame him past repenting — and find in it the pattern of how God deals with sinners, leaving the door open to the last. The evening that should have been all fellowship is shadowed by the presence of one whose heart is already sold, and the Lord meets that shadow not with exposure but with a final, unavailing offer of grace.

Pseudo-Jerome reads “the evening of the day” as “the evening of the world,” the last hour in which the labourers are called to their penny (Matthew 20:9); Theophylact resolves the question of posture — they reclined, the legal Passover first fulfilled. So the witnesses set the supper-scene with its undercurrent of grief: the Twelve at table, the Lord come to keep the feast, the betrayer among them not yet unmasked. The fathers dwell on Christ’s mingled mercy and warning — he says enough to give Judas room to repent, not so much as to shame him into haste — and find in it the pattern of God’s patience with sinners, leaving the door of return open to the very end. The evening of fellowship is shadowed by one sold heart, met not with exposure but with a last offer of grace.
18:
On Mark 14:18 — “And as they sat and did eat, Jesus said, Verily I say unto you, One of you which eateth with me shall betray me” — the witnesses feel the horror of betrayal at the sacred table.

The grammatical-historical Robertson insists on the true sense of the posture — “reclined, of course” — and laments that even Leonardo’s painting has them sitting. He fastens on “he that eateth with me” (cf. Psalm 41:9): “to this day the Arabs will not violate hospitality by mistreating one who breaks bread” with them — so monstrous is the betrayal of a table-companion. Ellicott corrects the rendering: “as they reclined.”

The Lord’s words are weighted with grief and with deliberate aim. He announces the treachery plainly enough to wound every conscience — “one of you” — yet leaves it veiled, that each may search himself. And he marks the aggravation that the betrayer is one “which eateth with me,” sharing the very bread of fellowship, fulfilling the ancient word of the friend who lifted up his heel against his benefactor.

So the witnesses gather the awful moment. At the table of the Passover, in the warmth of shared bread, the Lord names a traitor in the circle — and the naming is itself an act of mercy and of testimony. The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem, on the theme of fidelity and its reward, sets the faithful against the faithless. The verse teaches that no nearness to Christ, not even a place at his table, can sanctify a heart unsurrendered; and it sets the betrayer’s sin in its blackest frame — not the act of a stranger or an open enemy, but of a familiar friend who ate his bread and sold his life.

So the witnesses press the horror of betrayal at the table of fellowship. The Lord’s “one of you which eateth with me” (cf. Psalm 41:9) fulfils the ancient word of the trusted friend who lifted his heel against his benefactor; and the announcement, vague enough to leave each searching himself, is at once a wound to every conscience and a last testimony to the traitor. Robertson’s observation abides — that even among the Arabs the sharing of bread is a sacred bond, never to be violated — so that Judas’s sin is set in its blackest frame: not the act of a stranger or open foe, but of a familiar friend who dipped in the same dish and sold the Master whose bread he ate. No place at Christ’s table can sanctify an unsurrendered heart.
19:
On Mark 14:19 — “And they began to be sorrowful, and to say unto him one by one, Is it I? and another said, Is it I?” — the witnesses read the disciples’ grief and honest self-suspicion.

The voices here are brief. The Reformed Gill notes that “they began to be sorrowful… all but Judas, at this saying of Christ’s.” The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke observes the textual uncertainty of the closing clause, “and another said, Is it I?”, which several authorities omit. Pseudo-Jerome, on the previous verse, had heard in their questioning the harmony of a well-tuned harp, every string but one answering true.

The disciples’ response is itself a quiet testimony to their innocence. Stricken with sorrow at the word, they do not accuse one another, nor angrily protest, but each turns the question inward — “Is it I?” It is the instinct of true hearts, conscious of weakness if not of treachery, to fear above all that they might fail their Lord, and to submit themselves to his searching rather than to defend themselves.

So the witnesses commend the “one by one” of the asking. There is no clamour, no mutual suspicion, but a grave and personal self-examination passing round the table. Each man, save the one whose guilt was real, distrusts himself more than his neighbour, and brings his own heart to the judgement of Christ. The verse thus holds up a model for all who hear the warnings of the Lord: not to ask first “who else?” but “is it I?” — to receive every word of admonition as addressed to oneself, and to dread, more than any outward danger, the possibility of being found unfaithful to him who reads the heart.

So the witnesses commend the disciples’ instinct. Stricken with sorrow, they neither accuse one another nor angrily protest, but each turns the question inward — “Is it I?” It is the mark of true hearts, conscious of their weakness, to dread above all the failing of their Lord, and to submit themselves to his searching rather than defend themselves. Matthew records that Judas too must ask the question, lest his silence betray him; but the eleven ask it in earnest, one by one. The verse thus sets a model for all who hear the warnings of Christ: to receive every admonition as spoken to oneself, to answer “is it I?” before “is it he?” — for the heart that fears its own treachery is furthest from committing it, and the heart that never suspects itself is in the gravest danger.
20:
On Mark 14:20 — “It is one of the twelve, that dippeth with me in the dish” — the witnesses note the sign that yet left the traitor veiled.

The grammatical-historical Robertson observes how grievous the answer is — “it is as bad as that,” one of the chosen Twelve — and that “the sign that Jesus gave, the one dipping in the dish with me… escaped the notice of all”; Jesus gave the sop to Judas, “who understood perfectly that Jesus knew his purpose” (Matthew 26:21). The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke explains the Eastern custom: men “never eat together from one dish, except when a strong attachment subsists,” so that to dip with the Lord in the one dish marked a sharer of his closest fellowship — which made the treachery the worse.

The Lord’s reply both reveals and conceals. It names the betrayer as one of the inner circle, a man dipping his hand in the common dish with his Master — the very emblem of intimacy — yet so generally that the others still could not tell whom he meant. The sign was for Judas’s conscience, not for the table’s knowledge.

So the witnesses read in the answer a deepening of the horror rather than a plain exposure. Christ does not point the finger; he draws the circle close — one of the Twelve, one who eats from the same dish — and leaves the word to do its work in the one heart it concerns. The intimacy that should have bound Judas fast is the very measure of his sin: he betrays not a distant ruler but the Friend whose dish he shares. And still the door of repentance stands open, for the Lord exposes him to himself alone, that even now, if he would, he might turn back from the deed his heart has set.

So the witnesses read the answer as a deepening, not a disclosure. Christ does not name Judas to the table; he draws the circle close — “one of the twelve, that dippeth with me in the dish” — and leaves the word to do its work in the one heart it concerns. The shared dish, sign of closest fellowship, becomes the measure of the sin: the betrayer is no distant enemy but the intimate friend who reaches into the same bowl. Yet even here mercy lingers: by exposing Judas to himself alone, the Lord leaves the door of repentance ajar, that he might still turn back. The verse thus holds together the certainty of Christ’s knowledge and the patience of his love, naming the deed without destroying the doer’s last chance to forsake it.
21:
On Mark 14:21 — “The Son of man indeed goeth, as it is written of him: but woe to that man by whom the Son of man is betrayed! good were it for that man if he had never been born” — the witnesses hold together divine decree and human guilt.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the two clauses. Clarke glosses “goeth” as “to die” (cf. Matthew 26:24); Barnes links the woe to the warnings against causing offence (Matthew 18:7). Ellicott notes that “St. Mark… omits the fact recorded by St. Matthew, that the last ‘Is it I?’ was uttered by the Traitor.” The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem presses that Christ “came to His passion willingly… lest the whole world should perish in its sins.”

The verse sets, in a single breath, the appointed and the accountable. The Son of man “goeth as it is written” — his death no accident but the fulfilment of Scripture and the will of God; and yet “woe to that man” by whom it is wrought — the decree of God in no way lessening the guilt of the betrayer. The two truths stand together, unreconciled by any excuse for Judas.

So the witnesses refuse the false comfort that would palliate the traitor’s crime by appeal to prophecy. That Christ’s death was foreordained, and freely embraced by him for the world’s salvation, makes the betrayal no less wicked; Judas acted of his own will, and the “woe” falls full upon him. The terrible “good were it for that man if he had never been born” is the Lord’s own measure of the doom — spoken, the fathers note, not in vengeance but in grief, and even now within the betrayer’s hearing. The verse thus guards two pillars of the faith at once: the sovereign purpose that ordained the cross, and the real responsibility of the sinner who served it.

Barnes links the “woe” to the Lord’s solemn warnings against causing others to stumble (Matthew 18:7); Cyril of Jerusalem keeps the willingness of Christ in view, who “came to His passion” freely, “lest the whole world should perish.” So the witnesses hold the two truths in one hand. That the Son of man “goeth as it is written” sets his death within the eternal counsel of God and his own free obedience; that “woe” falls on the betrayer leaves the guilt of the deed entire. No appeal to prophecy excuses Judas, who acted of his own will; the decree that ordained the cross did not coerce the traitor’s heart. The terrible “good were it for that man if he had never been born” is grief, not vengeance — the Lord’s own measure of a doom freely chosen, spoken even now within the betrayer’s hearing.
22:
On Mark 14:22 — “Take, eat: this is my body” — the witnesses gather at the institution of the Lord’s Supper, where the schools divide most sharply.

The Eastern and Latin fathers read it with sacramental realism. Theophylact is most explicit: “the bread is not a mere figure of the Body of Christ, but is changed into the very Body of Christ” — though “veiled from our eyes… on account of our weakness.” Chrysostom: “it is not man who makes the offerings to be the Body and Blood… but Christ”; the priest speaks, but the words “This is My Body” consecrate “by the power and the grace of God.” Bede sets it under the oath, “Thou art a Priest for ever after the order of Melchizedek” (Psalm 110:4); Gregory marks that he “gave thanks” while taking on him “the stripes of other men’s wickedness.”

The Reformed and grammatical-historical voices, with the Puritan Henry, read it as memorial and sign. Henry: the Supper was “instituted… to be a memorial of Christ’s death,” food for the soul; Barnes refers to the bread of life (John 6:48–58) and Paul’s account (1 Corinthians 11:23); Gill calls the elements an emblem. Pseudo-Jerome stands between, reading a “mystical” transfiguration of the bread into the Body that is also “the present Church… broken in its sufferings.”

So the witnesses share one reverence and divide over the mode. The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem urges that communicants are “become of the same body and blood with Christ”; the fathers generally press a true participation in the Body, some to the point of a change of the elements, while the Reformed and evangelical voices hold a sign that seals union with Christ by faith and a memorial of his death “till his second coming.” Both confess that here, on the night of his betrayal, the Lord took bread, blessed and brake it, and gave himself to his own — the new covenant meal in which, by whatever understanding, Christ crucified is received and remembered.

Gregory draws a further lesson from the thanksgiving: that the sinless One, “taking upon Him the stripes of other men’s wickedness… humbly gives a blessing in His Passion,” teaching us what to do when we suffer for our own sins, since he bore so calmly the stripes due to ours. So the witnesses, divided over the mode, are agreed in the substance: here, on the night of his betrayal, the Lord takes bread, blesses, breaks, and gives himself to his own under the sign of bread. Whether received with the fathers’ realism — the very Body, veiled for our weakness — or with the Reformed as a sign and memorial sealing union by faith, the Supper sets forth Christ crucified, and bids his people feed on him with thanksgiving until he come.
23:
On Mark 14:23 — “And he took the cup, and when he had given thanks, he gave it to them: and they all drank of it” — the witnesses note the thanksgiving and the common cup.

The grammatical-historical commentators attend to the words and the drink. Ellicott marks that Mark, with Matthew, uses “blessing” of the bread and “giving thanks” of the cup, while Luke uses the latter of the bread and implies by “likewise” that the form was repeated. Robertson notes the cup held “the ordinary wine of the country mixed with… water,” though the Gospels here say not “wine” but “the fruit of the vine,” and that Mark and Matthew “give substantially the same account” as Paul (1 Corinthians 11:23). The Reformed Gill notes he gave thanks “over it, and for it, by blessing it, and appropriating it to the present… service.”

The detail “they all drank of it” is not idle. Where the bread was distributed, the cup is expressly said to be shared by all — a mark, the witnesses observe, of the common participation that the Supper enjoins, every disciple drinking of the one cup of the new covenant. The thanksgiving, too, is significant: the Lord blesses God over the very symbol of his outpoured blood.

So the witnesses gather the act of the cup beside the act of the bread. The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem again presses that by these mysteries the faithful are made one body and blood with Christ. And the giving of thanks, on the night of betrayal and before the agony, becomes a quiet wonder — that the Lord, taking up the cup of his suffering, should bless the Father over it. The verse joins the corporate “they all drank” to that thanksgiving, teaching that the Supper is at once a sharing of the one redemption among all his people and an offering of praise to God for the blood by which that redemption is sealed.

Robertson notes the cup held “the fruit of the vine” — the country’s wine mingled, by custom, with water — and that Mark’s account agrees with Paul’s received tradition (1 Corinthians 11:23). So the witnesses mark the deliberate “they all drank of it”: where the bread was distributed, the cup is expressly shared by every disciple, a sign of the common participation the Supper enjoins. And the thanksgiving over the symbol of his outpoured blood is itself a wonder — that the Lord, on the eve of the agony, should bless the Father over the cup of his own suffering. The Eastern Cyril presses that by these mysteries the faithful are “made of the same body and blood with Christ”; all confess that the one cup, received with thanks, binds the many into one and seals the new covenant in his blood.
24:
On Mark 14:24 — “This is my blood of the new testament, which is shed for many” — the witnesses expound the covenant and the scope of “many.”

The grammatical-historical Ellicott corrects the rendering — “is being shed,” the participle being present tense both here and in Matthew — the blood even now in the act of outpouring. Barnes points to the cup of communion in the blood (1 Corinthians 10:16; John 6:53). The Reformed Calvin presses that the words “direct us to the sacrifice of the death of Christ, without the remembrance of which the Lord’s Supper is never observed in a proper manner,” for souls can only be satisfied “by being assured that God is pacified towards them”; and by “many” Christ means “not a part of the world only.”

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley reads the cup as “a perpetual sign and memorial of my blood, as shed for establishing the new covenant, that all who shall believe in me may receive all its gracious promises.” The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem finds the mystical table of Psalm 23:5 set, since Christ’s coming, in place of the table of devils.

So the witnesses confess together the heart of the Supper: the cup is the blood of the covenant, poured out for the remission of sins. The blood is “of the new testament” — the new covenant ratified, as the old at Sinai, in sacrificial blood; and it is shed “for many,” a word the witnesses take in its fulness of saving scope. Whether read with the fathers’ realism or the Reformers’ remembrance, the verse fixes the meaning of the cup on the sacrifice of Christ’s death; and Calvin’s caution abides for all — that the Supper is rightly kept only where the blood there signified is received in faith as the ground of peace with God.

So the witnesses fix the meaning of the cup on the sacrifice of the cross. “The blood of the new testament” is the new covenant ratified, as the old at Sinai, in sacrificial blood; “shed for many” — a word Calvin takes in its fulness, “not a part of the world only” — declares the saving reach of the offering “for the remission of sins.” Calvin’s caution abides for every communicant: the Supper directs us “to the sacrifice of the death of Christ,” and is rightly kept only where the soul is “assured that God is pacified” through that blood. Whether read with the realism of the fathers or the remembrance of the Reformers, the cup proclaims one truth — that the blood of the covenant, poured out for many, is the ground of peace with God.
25:
On Mark 14:25 — “I will drink no more of the fruit of the vine, until that day that I drink it new in the kingdom of God” — the witnesses hear the farewell and the pledge of the great reunion.

The grammatical-historical commentators read it plainly and forward. Ellicott notes “the product” of the vine, and the difference between Mark’s “the kingdom of God” and Matthew’s “the kingdom of My Father.” Barnes sets the “new” drinking beside the prophets’ pictures of the kingdom’s abundance (Joel 3:18; Amos 9:13). The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley paraphrases: “I shall drink no more before I die: the next wine I drink will not be earthly, but heavenly.”

The saying joins a solemn parting to a sure hope. This is the last cup the Lord will share with his own on earth; the next, he promises, will be drunk “new” in the consummated kingdom. The shadow of imminent death lies over the words — “I will drink no more” — yet they reach past the cross to the feast beyond it.

So the witnesses find in the verse both the closing of the old fellowship and the betrothal to a new. The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem, on the theme of eternal life, gathers the promise into the hope of the kingdom. The Lord does not end on the note of his going, but on that of the day when he and his shall drink together in the joy of the Father’s reign. The broken company of this night — soon to be scattered, the cup soon to be exchanged for the bitter cup of Gethsemane — is given, in this word, the assurance of an unbroken table to come, where the wine of the kingdom shall be poured and the Lord himself will share it with his redeemed.

The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem gathers the promise into the hope of eternal life in the kingdom. So the witnesses hear in the verse the closing of one fellowship and the betrothal to another. This is the last cup the Lord will share with his own on earth; the shadow of imminent death lies on “I will drink no more.” Yet the word reaches past the cross to the feast beyond it — the wine “new” in the kingdom of God, the prophets’ pictured abundance (Joel 3:18) fulfilled. The company about to be scattered, the cup about to be exchanged for the bitter cup of Gethsemane, is given here the pledge of an unbroken table to come, where the Lord himself will drink with his redeemed in the joy of the Father’s reign.
26:
On Mark 14:26 — “And when they had sung an hymn, they went out into the mount of Olives” — the witnesses mark the closing psalm and the move toward the agony.

The Eastern and Latin fathers draw lessons from the hymn and the going out. Theophylact: as they gave thanks before drinking, “so they return thanks after… to teach us to return thanks both before and after our food,” and shows that Christ “was glad to die for us” in deigning to praise God when about to be betrayed. Pseudo-Jerome takes the “hymn” as the praise of the Psalms (Psalm 22:26). Bede recalls the great prayer of John 17, and finds the Lord leading them to Olivet to teach that “through the reception of the Sacraments we ought to rise up” to higher things.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify the song. Gill, Lightfoot, and Robertson name it the Hallel (Psalms 113–118) sung at the Passover. The Reformed Calvin notes that the three Evangelists “leave out those divine discourses” of John 14–17, glancing only at the fact that Christ “went out of his own accord” to meet his enemies.

So the witnesses set the hymn at the hinge of the night. The supper ends not in fear but in praise — the Lord and his own singing the ancient psalms of deliverance even as the betrayer hastens to the priests. Augustine here takes up the difficulty of Peter’s denial, which Mark alone relates “more minutely,” noting the cock-crowing; and the company passes out to the Mount of Olives, toward Gethsemane. The verse joins worship to the threshold of suffering: with a song on his lips the Saviour goes to his agony, teaching his people that thanksgiving belongs not only to the table of joy but to the very entrance of the valley of sorrow.

Lightfoot supplies the rabbinic rule that bound the Passover to its hymn, and Augustine takes occasion, from Mark’s fuller note of the cock-crowing, to harmonise the accounts of Peter’s threefold denial. So the witnesses set the hymn at the hinge of the night. The supper ends not in fear but in praise: the Lord and his own sing the ancient psalms of deliverance even as the betrayer hastens to the priests, and then pass out to the Mount of Olives toward the garden. Theophylact’s lesson stands — that thanksgiving belongs both before and after our food, and that Christ, “glad to die for us,” deigned to praise God on the eve of his betrayal. With a song on his lips the Saviour goes to his agony, teaching that worship belongs even at the entrance of the valley of sorrow.
27:
On Mark 14:27 — “All ye shall be offended because of me this night: for it is written, I will smite the shepherd, and the sheep shall be scattered” — the witnesses read the forewarning of the disciples’ fall.

The grammatical-historical Barnes grounds the citation in Zechariah 13:7 — the smitten shepherd, the scattered flock. The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley dwells on “this night,” noting the Jewish reckoning of the day from evening (Genesis 1:5), so that what is here “this night” is in verse 30 “to-day”; the prediction is the more pointed for its nearness. The Reformed Gill places the saying “as they were going to the Mount of Olives.”

The Lord foretells not the treachery of one but the failure of all: “all ye shall be offended… this night.” The prophecy of Zechariah is fulfilled in him — the Shepherd struck, the sheep scattered — and the disciples’ coming flight is set within the appointed pattern of the Passion, foretold that, when it came, it might not overthrow their faith past recovery.

So the witnesses hear in the warning a severe mercy. Christ does not spare his own the knowledge of their weakness; he tells them plainly, before it happens, that they will all stumble that very night. Yet the citation of Scripture frames their fall not as a surprise that defeats God’s purpose but as a thing written, even as the Shepherd’s smiting was written. And the next verse will turn the prophecy toward hope: the same God who foretold the scattering foretold the gathering. The forewarning thus prepares them to read their own failure, when it comes, not as the end of all but as a stumbling within the sovereign plan that the risen Shepherd will redeem.

So the witnesses hear in the warning a severe mercy. The Lord does not spare his own the knowledge of their weakness, but tells them plainly that “this night” they will all be offended — the smitten Shepherd, the scattered sheep of Zechariah 13:7. Wesley marks how near the word presses: what is here “this night” is, by the Jewish reckoning of the day from evening (Genesis 1:5), the very “to-day” of the next verses. Yet the citation of Scripture frames their fall not as a defeat of God’s purpose but as a thing foretold, even as the Shepherd’s smiting was foretold; and the next breath turns it toward hope. The warning thus prepares the disciples to read their coming failure, when it comes, as a stumbling within the sovereign plan their risen Lord will redeem.
28:
On Mark 14:28 — “But after that I am risen, I will go before you into Galilee” — the witnesses hear, amid the warning, a word of comfort and promise.

The voices here are brief but sure. The Reformed Gill marks the tender purpose: Christ assures them of his rising “for their comfort… though they would be offended and discouraged” by his seizing and crucifixion. The grammatical-historical Barnes points to the fulfilment — the risen Lord did go before them into Galilee (Mark 16:7) — so that the promise was both a consolation and a tryst.

The placing of the promise is everything. It falls between the prophecy of the scattering and its accomplishment: before the sheep are scattered, the Shepherd tells them where he will meet them again. “After that I am risen” — the resurrection is spoken of as certain, the gathering as sure as the scattering. The flock will be broken for a night, but not lost; the Shepherd, smitten and risen, will go before them still.

So the witnesses find the gospel lodged in the very heart of the warning. The Lord does not leave his failing disciples without hope; even as he foretells their flight, he promises a reunion in Galilee, where their discipleship had begun. The angel at the empty tomb would one day repeat these very words (Mark 16:7), recalling the promise the disciples had forgotten. The verse teaches that Christ’s foreknowledge of our falls is never the last word: beyond the smiting lies the rising, and beyond the scattering, the gathering — the same Shepherd who knew they would forsake him appointing, in advance, the place of their restoration.

So the witnesses find the gospel lodged in the very heart of the warning. Between the prophecy of the scattering and its fulfilment the Shepherd appoints the place of reunion: “after that I am risen, I will go before you into Galilee” (Mark 16:7). The resurrection is spoken of as certain, the gathering as sure as the scattering; the flock will be broken for a night but not lost. The angel at the empty tomb would one day repeat these very words, recalling the promise the frightened disciples had forgotten. The verse teaches that Christ’s foreknowledge of our falls is never his last word: beyond the smiting lies the rising, and beyond the scattering, the gathering — the Shepherd who knew they would forsake him appointing, before they fled, the very place of their restoration.
29:
On Mark 14:29 — “But Peter said unto him, Although all shall be offended, yet will not I” — the witnesses read Peter’s well-meant but presumptuous boast.

The voices here are brief. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes that “Mark records here Peter’s boast of loyalty even though all desert him,” and that “all the Gospels tell it” (cf. Matthew 26:33). The Reformed Gill marks Peter “greatly moved” at the word that all would be offended and scattered, and so protesting his own constancy above the rest.

The boast is characteristic of Peter — warm, sincere, and over-confident. He does not deny that the others may fail; he excepts only himself, setting his own loyalty above his brethren’s. The very form of his protest, “yet will not I,” betrays the self-reliance that the night will expose.

So the witnesses see in Peter both the genuineness of love and the peril of pride. His attachment to Christ was real; his estimate of his own strength was false. To claim a steadfastness greater than all the rest, in the teeth of his Master’s plain word, was to trust himself where he should have trembled. The verse stands as a warning to every confident disciple: that the heart may be sincere and yet deceived, that love untaught by self-knowledge is no guarantee against falling, and that the surest way to the denial Peter so dreaded was the very self-confidence with which he here disowned the possibility of it.

The boast is wholly characteristic of Peter — warm, sincere, and over-confident. He does not deny that the others may stumble; he excepts himself alone, setting his loyalty above his brethren’s in the teeth of his Master’s plain word. Robertson notes that all four Gospels record the boast (cf. Matthew 26:33; John 13:37), so signal was its later reversal. So the witnesses find in Peter both the genuineness of love and the peril of self-trust. His attachment to Christ was real; his estimate of his own strength was false; and to claim a steadfastness beyond all the rest was to lean on himself where he should have trembled and prayed. The verse stands as a warning to every confident disciple: sincere affection, untaught by self-knowledge, is no safeguard against falling, and the loudest vow of constancy may be the nearest neighbour to the fall.
30:
On Mark 14:30 — “Verily I say unto thee, That this day, even in this night, before the cock crow twice, thou shalt deny me thrice” — the witnesses mark the precision peculiar to Mark.

The grammatical-historical commentators fasten on the “twice.” Robertson notes this detail is “only in Mark” — “one crowing is always the signal for more” — and that the cock-crowing “marks the third watch of the night” (Mark 13:35). Ellicott judges “twice” the true reading, agreeing with the “second time” of Mark 14:72, the omission in some manuscripts being “accidental.” Clarke marks how “deeply emphatical” every word is: “Thou, this day, in this very night, before the cock shall crow twice, thou wilt deny me.”

The Lord answers Peter’s boast with a prediction of pinpoint exactness. Not only will Peter be offended like the rest; he will deny his Master outright, three times, and that within hours — before the cock has crowed twice. The very precision is a kindness and a proof: a foresight so detailed could come only from One who knew all hearts, and its fulfilment would one day break Peter to repentance.

So the witnesses contrast the disciple’s confidence with the Master’s knowledge. Peter says “not I”; Christ says “thou, this night, thrice.” The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem, on the theme of Christ’s sovereign “Verily I say unto thee,” recalls the like word of certainty to the penitent thief. The exactness of the prediction — the day, the night, the number, the double cock-crow — marks the speaker as more than man, and turns the coming failure, when it comes, into a summons home rather than a sentence: the same Lord who foretold the denial had already, the Gospels will show, prayed that Peter’s faith should not fail utterly, but be restored.

So the witnesses contrast the disciple’s confidence with the Master’s knowledge. Peter says “not I”; Christ answers “thou, this night, thrice, before the cock crow twice.” The very exactness — the day, the night, the number, the double cock-crow that Mark alone records — marks the speaker as more than man, reading the heart that even its owner did not know. Cyril of Jerusalem recalls the like sovereign “Verily I say unto thee” spoken to the penitent thief. And the prediction, terrible as it is, is also mercy: a foresight so precise, once fulfilled, would break Peter to repentance rather than to despair, for the same Lord who foretold the denial had, the Gospels show, already prayed that his faith should not fail utterly but be restored to strengthen his brethren.
31:
On Mark 14:31 — “But he spake the more vehemently, If I should die with thee, I will not deny thee in any wise. Likewise also said they all” — the witnesses note the redoubled boast.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the vehemence. Barnes: “more earnestly, more confidently”; Ellicott notes the Greek tense “implies frequent and continuous speaking”; Robertson observes the strong compounded adverb “exceeding vehemently” (ekperissōs), found “only in Mark and probably… preserves Peter’s own statement.” The Reformed Gill describes him speaking “with a louder voice; with more spirit and eagerness; in a more peremptory and self-confident way.”

Far from being checked by the Lord’s precise prediction, Peter redoubles his protest, now binding it with the strongest pledge: though he should die, he will not deny. And the rest, the witnesses note, joined him — “likewise also said they all.” The whole company, in sincere but ignorant zeal, vowed a fidelity that the night would shatter.

So the witnesses set the loud, united vow against the silence into which the disciples’ courage would so soon collapse. Peter’s “in any wise” is the high-water mark of his self-confidence; within hours he will swear he never knew the man. The detail that “they all” said the same spreads the lesson beyond Peter: it was not one man’s peculiar fault but the common weakness of flesh that overrated itself. The verse thus prepares the reader for the flight and the denial, and presses home the warning of the whole passage — that the warmest professions, made in our own strength and against the word of Christ, are no defence in the hour of trial, but often the very prelude to the fall they so loudly disown.

So the witnesses set the loud, united vow against the silence into which the disciples’ courage would so soon collapse. Peter’s “in any wise” is the high-water mark of his self-confidence; within hours he will swear he never knew the man. And the note that “they all” said the same spreads the lesson beyond him: it was no peculiar fault of Peter’s but the common weakness of flesh overrating itself. The verse thus prepares the reader for the flight and the denial, and presses the warning of the whole passage — that the warmest professions, made in our own strength and against the word of Christ, are no defence in the hour of trial. Often, indeed, they are its very prelude, the confidence that will not watch and pray being the confidence that falls.
32:
On Mark 14:32 — “And they came to a place which was named Gethsemane: and he saith to his disciples, Sit ye here, while I shall pray” — the witnesses enter the garden of the agony.

The Latin and Eastern fathers expound the place and the prayer. Bede explains Gethsemane as “the valley of… fatness”: praying on a mountain he teaches us to ask lofty things, and in the valley to keep “humility and the fatness of interior love.” Theophylact notes it was Christ’s “custom always to pray by Himself… to seek for silence and solitude in our prayers,” and that he took the three who had seen his glory on the mount, that they who saw his glory might see his sufferings and learn “that He is really man.” Augustine guards the words to come, “if it be possible”: not that the Father lacked power, but “if He is willing.”
