The grammatical-historical Robertson and Ellicott render the charge precisely — “sit ye here… till I shall have prayed.” The Puritan Henry marks that here Christ “begins with those which were the sorest of all his sufferings, those in his soul,” and that “no sorrow was forced upon him, but… he freely admitted” it.

So the witnesses open the agony with reverence and care. The Lord withdraws to a familiar garden to pray, leaving the body of the disciples at its edge and taking three within. The fathers find in the scene both example and mystery: the example of solitary, humble prayer, and the mystery of the Son of God truly sorrowful as man. Gloss notes the Evangelist now turns from the foretold offence of the disciples to the Lord’s prayer, “in which He is supposed to have prayed for His disciples.” The valley of fatness becomes the place of the soul’s deepest anguish — and the entrance, the witnesses will show, of that obedience by which the world is saved.

Pseudo-Jerome marks that the disciples are “separated from Him in prayer, who are separated in His Passion; for He prays, they sleep” — and that we learn here to fear and be sorrowful before the judgement of death. So the witnesses open the agony with reverence: the Lord withdraws to a familiar garden, leaves the body of the disciples at its edge, and takes three within to share, so far as they can, his vigil. The fathers find in the scene both example and mystery — the example of solitary, humble prayer (the “valley of fatness” that keeps humility and interior love), and the mystery of the Son of God truly sorrowful as man. The place of olives, where the press crushes out the oil, becomes the place where the Saviour’s soul is pressed in the beginning of that obedience by which the world is saved.
33:
On Mark 14:33 — “And he taketh with him Peter and James and John, and began to be sore amazed, and to be very heavy” — the witnesses measure the depth of the Lord’s anguish.

The grammatical-historical commentators fasten on Mark’s stronger words. Ellicott notes “St. Mark’s use of the stronger word” (“sore amazed”) as against Matthew’s “sorrowful.” Robertson observes that Mark alone uses ekthambeisthai (also at Mark 9:15), and that “now Jesus himself feels amazement as he directly faces the struggle.” The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley: the word “imports the most shocking amazement, mingled with grief,” the next term meaning he was “surrounded with sorrow on every side… ready to separate his soul from his body.” The Reformed Gill marks that the three taken were those who had seen the transfiguration and now see his sorrows.

The verse presses the reality of Christ’s human dread to its furthest. He who had moved with such calm toward Jerusalem now “begins to be sore amazed”; the horror of what he faces breaks over his soul. Mark, blunter than the others, will not soften it: the Lord is appalled and overwhelmed.

So the witnesses behold, with awe, the true manhood of the Saviour in his agony. The three who beheld his glory on the mount are brought to behold his anguish in the garden, that they might know him very man as well as very God. His amazement is no failure of faith but the genuine recoil of a sinless human nature before the cup of wrath it is about to drink. The verse forbids every docetic lightening of the Passion: the One who saves us entered fully into our dread, and bore, in a real human soul, a sorrow that brought him to the edge of death — that he might redeem us from within the very nature he had taken.

Robertson notes that the same verb of amazement had described the disciples’ awe as Jesus went up toward Jerusalem (Mark 10:32); “now Jesus himself feels amazement as he directly faces the struggle.” So the witnesses behold, with awe, the true manhood of the Saviour in his agony. The three who beheld his glory on the mount are brought to behold his anguish in the garden, that they might know him very man as well as very God. His “sore amazement” is no failure of faith but the genuine recoil of a sinless human nature before the cup of wrath. The verse forbids every lightening of the Passion: the One who saves us entered fully into our dread, and bore in a real human soul a sorrow that brought him to the edge of death, that he might redeem us from within the nature he had taken.
34:
On Mark 14:34 — “My soul is exceeding sorrowful unto death: tarry ye here, and watch” — the witnesses dwell on the depth of the sorrow and the plea for company.

The voices here are brief but weighty. The grammatical-historical Barnes refers the saying to the Lord’s own words in John 12:27, “now is my soul troubled” — the same trouble of soul, here deepened “unto death.” The Reformed Gill notes the words were spoken to “the above three disciples,” whom he had drawn apart to share, so far as they could, his vigil.

“Exceeding sorrowful unto death” is the Lord’s own measure of his agony. It is not a passing grief but a sorrow so crushing that, as the commentators on the parallel observe, it pressed him to the very gates of death — as though the weight alone might have parted soul from body. And to this is joined a plea that is wholly human: “tarry ye here, and watch” — the craving of the suffering Christ for the nearness of his friends.

So the witnesses set the two halves of the verse side by side: the immeasurable sorrow and the simple request. The Saviour, entering the darkest hour of his soul, asks of the three only that they stay near and keep awake with him. It was not too much to ask; yet, as the night will show, even this they could not do. The verse thus deepens the wonder of the Passion — that the Lord of glory should be brought so low as to long for the watching of weak men — and prepares the poignant contrast to come, when the friends he asked to watch will sleep, and he will tread the winepress alone.

So the witnesses set the two halves of the verse side by side: an immeasurable sorrow and a simple request. “Exceeding sorrowful unto death” is the Lord’s own measure of his agony — the trouble of soul of John 12:27 now deepened to the very gates of death, as though the weight alone might have parted soul from body. And to this he joins a wholly human plea: “tarry ye here, and watch” — the craving of the suffering Christ for the nearness of his friends. It was not too much to ask; yet, as the night would show, even this the three could not do. The verse deepens the wonder of the Passion, that the Lord of glory should be brought so low as to long for the watching of weak men, and prepares the poignant contrast of the sleeping disciples and the Saviour’s lonely prayer.
35:
On Mark 14:35 — “And he went forward a little, and fell on the ground, and prayed that, if it were possible, the hour might pass from him” — the witnesses behold the Lord prostrate in prayer.

The grammatical-historical Robertson reads the descriptive imperfects: “fell on the ground” — “see him falling” — and “prayed,” whether “repeatedly” or “began to pray.” He notes “the hour” that Jesus had “long looked forward to” and often mentioned (John 12:27); “now he dreads it, surely a human trait that all can understand.” The Reformed Gill marks that he “went forward a little,” about a stone’s cast, and Barnes refers to the prayer “with strong crying and tears” (Hebrews 5:7).

The posture itself speaks. The Son of God falls to the ground — not kneeling only but prostrate — in an abasement that matches the greatness of his sorrow. And the prayer is real: “if it were possible, the hour might pass.” The very hour he had set his face toward, he now shrinks from, in the genuine recoil of a human will before the cup of wrath.

So the witnesses find in the falling and the praying the lowest point of the Saviour’s humiliation thus far. There is no pretence in it, no mere show of feeling: he dreads the hour, and pours out that dread to the Father. Yet the prayer is hedged with “if it were possible,” and will be crowned, in the next verse, with “nevertheless, not what I will.” The verse thus shows us the true wrestling of the God-man — a real fear, a real prayer for release — held within a real submission. He prays as we must pray, from the dust, with the whole weight of our nature’s shrinking from death; and in so praying he sanctifies the prayers of all his people who must one day face their own dark hour.

Robertson marks that “the hour” is the very hour Jesus had long looked toward and often named (John 12:27); “now he dreads it, surely a human trait that all can understand.” So the witnesses find in the falling and the praying the lowest point of the Saviour’s humiliation thus far. The Son of God lies prostrate on the ground, in an abasement matching the greatness of his sorrow, and prays in earnest — “with strong crying and tears” (Hebrews 5:7) — that, “if it were possible, the hour might pass.” There is no pretence in it; he truly shrinks from the cup of wrath. Yet the prayer is hedged with “if it were possible,” and will be crowned with “nevertheless, not what I will” — the real fear held within a real submission, sanctifying the prayers of all his people who must face their own dark hour.
36:
On Mark 14:36 — “Abba, Father, all things are possible unto thee; take away this cup from me: nevertheless not what I will, but what thou wilt” — the witnesses dwell on the cry of the agony and its perfect submission.

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the double word “Abba, Father.” Robertson notes both Aramaic and Greek, each with the article — “not a case of translation, but the use of both terms” as in Galatians 4:6, “a probable memory of Paul’s childhood prayers”; and that the language cannot be taken as fear of dying before the cross, for “he was heard” (Hebrews 5:7) and helped to submit. Ellicott marks that the record of “Abba” as actually uttered is “peculiar to St. Mark,” its impress felt in Romans 8:15. Barnes and Clarke note the word denotes “filial affection and tenderness” (cf. Romans 8:15; Galatians 4:6). Lightfoot distinguishes the Hebrew and Chaldee senses, “Abba” denoting “only a natural father… My father.”

The prayer holds, in one breath, the fullest faith and the fullest surrender. “All things are possible unto thee” — the Father could remove the cup; “take away this cup from me” — the true human shrinking asks it; “nevertheless not what I will, but what thou wilt” — the will of the Son bows wholly to the Father’s. Wesley notes Mark adds the explanatory “Father” to the Aramaic “Abba.”

So the witnesses find in this verse the very heart of Gethsemane and the pattern of all true prayer. The cup is the cup of wrath, and the Saviour does not feign indifference to it; he asks, with childlike trust in a Father to whom all things are possible, that it pass. Yet he asks within an absolute “nevertheless,” subjecting his human will to the divine. Here, the fathers and the Reformers agree, is no reluctant fate dragging him to the cross, but a free obedience offered from the dust. The “Abba” that broke from his lips in that hour became, by the Spirit, the cry of every adopted child (Romans 8:15); and the “not what I will, but what thou wilt” is the prayer by which the world was saved, and the model by which his people learn to pray.
